Before buying any packaged snack, find the 'Added Sugars' line on the nutrition label. If it's over 10g, put it back and choose an alternative.
Replace the evening dessert with a cup of peppermint or cinnamon tea. The ritual of a warm drink provides the 'closure' to the meal without the glucose spike.

goal: Engage in 15 minutes of progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) daily
description: Ananya is a 31-year-old corporate lawyer living in a high-density urban apartment with constant construction noise. She suffers from 'generalized anxiety' and carries extreme tension in her jaw and shoulders, leading to daily tension headaches. She finds traditional 'clear your mind' meditation frustrating because her thoughts race toward her billable hours. Her main friction point is the feeling that sitting still is 'unproductive' and the constant startle response triggered by outside noises.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Often works until 8 PM; shared living space with a partner who also works from home.
baseline.physical_constraints: TMJ (jaw clicking) and chronic shoulder knots; cannot sit in one position for more than 20 minutes.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use noise-canceling headphones; session must occur before the evening 'email check' to lower cortisol.

tasks.description: Put on noise-canceling headphones and play a low-frequency brown noise track. This masks the construction sounds that trigger her startle reflex.
Focus exclusively on the jaw, neck, and shoulders. Tense each muscle group for 5 seconds and release for 10 seconds. Repeat 3 times per group.
Progress to tensing calves and feet. If she feels restless, she is allowed to do this while standing or pacing slowly, as long as the focus remains on the muscle tension-release cycle.
Note the intensity of her current headache on a 1-10 scale in a bedside journal. This creates a data-driven link between the habit and physical relief, satisfying her 'productivity' drive.

goal: Build a personal digital library of 50 organized reference snippets per month
description: Liam is a 24-year-old junior content creator who feels constantly overwhelmed by 'browser tab debt.' He currently has 45+ tabs open across three windows, which slows down his computer and his focus. He fears losing a 'great idea' if he closes a tab. His main friction point is the 'organization tax'—he hates the manual effort of tagging and filing, leading to a chaotic desktop that triggers his executive dysfunction.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited cloud storage; uses a single monitor which makes split-screen work difficult.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but high digital eye-fatigue.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a centralized tool (like Notion or Obsidian); must close all 'non-essential' tabs by the end of each workday.

tasks.description: Every day at 4:30 PM, look at every open tab. If it hasn't been touched in 2 hours, it must either be bookmarked into a 'To Process' folder or closed immediately.
For every snippet saved, write exactly one sentence explaining why it matters (e.g., 'Use this lighting technique for the June video'). This prevents hoarding useless data.
Physically close the browser window entirely. The goal is to start the next morning with a blank 'Home' page to reduce morning overwhelm.
On Friday, delete 5 saved items that no longer seem relevant. This builds the habit of 'curation' over 'collection'.

goal: Cook and eat a protein-heavy breakfast within 30 minutes of waking
description: Simone is a 42-year-old pharmaceutical sales rep who spends her mornings in her car. She usually skips breakfast, relies on black coffee until 2 PM, and then overeats at dinner due to extreme hunger. She experiences 'brain fog' during her morning presentations. Her friction point is her dislike of 'morning smells' like frying eggs and the limited time she has before hitting the road to avoid traffic.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be on the road by 7:15 AM; no access to a microwave during the day.
baseline.physical_constraints: Occasional acid reflux triggered by skipping meals.
baseline.non_negotiables: Breakfast must contain at least 25g of protein; prep time must be under 5 minutes in the morning.

tasks.description: On Sunday, hard-boil 10 eggs and portion out 5 containers of Greek yogurt with protein powder. This eliminates the 'cooking smells' she dislikes on weekday mornings.
She cannot take the first sip of coffee until she has consumed at least half of the prepared protein breakfast. This uses her coffee 'addiction' as a positive anchor.
Keep two high-protein shakes in the car console. If she is running late and misses the home breakfast, she must finish the shake before arriving at her first client meeting.
At 11:00 AM, send herself a quick text rating her focus (1-5). Seeing '5' on days she ate breakfast will reinforce the habit.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing basic piano scales and chord transitions
description: David is a 68-year-old retiree who bought a keyboard to 'keep his brain sharp.' He has early signs of osteoarthritis in his fingers. He often feels discouraged because his progress is slow and his fingers feel 'stiff and clumsy.' His friction point is 'Self-Criticism'—he expects to sound like a professional and gets frustrated by simple mistakes, leading him to avoid the keyboard for days at a time.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Grandchildren visit on Wednesdays (loud environment); uses a tablet for sheet music.
baseline.physical_constraints: Finger joint stiffness; cannot play for more than 15 minutes without discomfort.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a heating pad for hands prior to playing; practice must be 'low volume' to avoid bothering neighbors.

tasks.description: Use a warm compress or heating pad on both hands for 5 minutes. This reduces the 'clumsy' feeling caused by osteoarthritis and makes the habit more pleasant.
Play a simple C-major scale up and down 5 times, focusing on keeping the wrist level. If a mistake is made, he must smile and continue—no restarting allowed.
Set a timer for 2 minutes. Switch between a C-major and G-major chord as many times as possible. This builds muscle memory without the pressure of a full song.
End the session by playing one 'free-form' sequence of notes that sounds good to him, regardless of technical accuracy. This ends the habit on a positive emotional note.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily performing post-injury ankle mobility drills
description: Keira is a 19-year-old varsity soccer player recovering from a Grade 2 lateral ankle sprain. She is eager to return to the field and tends to over-train, which causes swelling. She finds the 'slow' rehab exercises boring compared to a 90-minute practice. Her friction point is 'Inconsistency'—she does the drills when it hurts, but stops as soon as the pain subsides, leading to chronic instability.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Full class schedule; must attend team meetings (sedentary); uses a resistance band for rehab.
baseline.physical_constraints: Residual swelling; sharp pain if moved into extreme inversion.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use ice for 10 minutes after any drill; no running until cleared by the trainer.

tasks.description: While sitting in her first morning lecture, trace the entire alphabet in the air with her big toe. This improves range of motion without needing equipment.
Sit on the floor and use the band to pull the foot outward (15 reps, 3 sets). Focus on slow, controlled movement to build the stabilizing peroneal muscles.
While brushing teeth, stand on the injured leg for 30 seconds, then 30 seconds on the other. This integrates proprioception (balance) training into a non-negotiable daily habit.
Prop the foot up on two pillows and apply an ice pack. This is the 'cooldown' that prevents the swelling she currently ignores.

goal: Review and update all personal insurance beneficiaries and emergency contacts within 30 days
description: Linda is a 62-year-old recent divorcee who hasn't looked at her 401k or life insurance details in over a decade. Her ex-husband is still listed as the primary beneficiary on several accounts. She finds the legal jargon in these documents terrifying and often feels a 'brain fog' of anxiety when she tries to log into her banking portals. Her main friction point is the emotional weight of revisiting old documents while still processing her separation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited tech literacy; relies on a physical filing cabinet for most records; works part-time at a library.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but experiences heart palpitations when dealing with financial legalities.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a password manager for security; cannot finish more than one account review per session to avoid burnout.

tasks.description: Open the physical filing cabinet and pull out exactly one folder related to insurance or banking. Do not read the contents yet; simply place it on the desk.
Select one digital account and successfully log in. If the password is forgotten, go through the reset process. Once inside, log back out immediately to build confidence with the portal.

Locate the 'Beneficiaries' section of the logged-in account. Write down the name currently listed on a piece of paper. If it needs changing, click 'Edit' but do not commit changes until the next day to ensure no emotional errors.
After closing the laptop, spend 5 minutes listening to a favorite song or drinking tea. This positive reinforcement helps decouple financial tasks from the feeling of dread.

goal: Sort and discard 15 bags of unnecessary household items to downsize
description: Arthur is a 74-year-old widower living in a four-bedroom house he has occupied for 40 years. He is planning to move to a smaller assisted-living apartment. He is a 'sentimental saver' and finds it physically painful to throw away objects associated with his late wife. His friction point is 'Stall Fatigue'—he starts sorting a box, gets lost in memories for two hours, and ends up not throwing anything away.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited mobility; cannot carry heavy bags down the stairs alone; requires a pickup service for donations.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronic arthritis in knees; cannot stand for more than 15 minutes at a time.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must keep a 'Memory Box' for items he cannot bear to lose; no more than one bag of trash/donation per session.

tasks.description: Bring one small box of items to his favorite armchair. Sit comfortably with a trash bag on the left and a 'keep' box on the right.

Pick up an item. If it’s junk (old mail, broken pens), it goes in the bag within 3 seconds. If it’s sentimental, it goes in the 'keep' box immediately to be processed later. This prevents the 'memory stall'.
Once a bag is half-full, use a rolling walker to transport it to the front door for his son to take out later. This avoids heavy lifting while still making progress.
For one item per session that he is discarding but wants to remember, take a quick photo with his phone. This allows him to 'keep the memory without the clutter'.

goal: Walk 30 minutes daily using an interval-based 'Couch to 5k' approach
description: Maya is a 35-year-old mother of twins who is currently 50 lbs overweight. She was athletic in high school but hasn't exercised in years. She feels 'visible' and self-conscious when walking in her neighborhood. Her friction point is 'all-or-nothing' thinking—if she can't run the whole way, she feels like a failure and quits. She also deals with plantar fasciitis in her left heel.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Childcare ends at 8 PM; morning routine starts at 6 AM; limited budget for high-end running shoes.
baseline.physical_constraints: Heel pain (plantar fasciitis); requires specific stretching before any walking.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must wear supportive sneakers; must stop immediately if heel pain exceeds a 3/10 scale.

tasks.description: Perform 3 rounds of calf stretches against a wall (30 seconds each side) to alleviate tension in the plantar fascia before putting on shoes.
Walk a 10-minute loop around the block during the 'golden hour' (dusk) when fewer neighbors are out, reducing her social anxiety.
Every 3 minutes of normal walking, increase speed to a 'power walk' for 30 seconds. This builds cardiovascular strength without the impact of running.
After the walk, roll the arch of her left foot over a frozen water bottle for 5 minutes. This manages inflammation and acts as a 'cooldown' ritual.

goal: Implement a 3-tier 'Digital Sunset' to improve deep sleep within 14 days
description: Toby is a 19-year-old competitive e-sports player who suffers from 'Revenge Bedtime Procrastination.' He feels his only 'free time' is after his parents go to bed, so he stays on Discord and gaming until 4 AM. He wakes up with 'burning' eyes and extreme irritability. His friction point is 'Social FOMO'—he feels he will lose his rank or his friends if he logs off early.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shared bedroom; requires high-speed internet for matches; high ambient light from PC setup.
baseline.physical_constraints: Digital eye strain; occasional migraines.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be off all competitive ranked matches by 11 PM; no blue light exposure after midnight.

tasks.description: Commit to no new ranked match starts after 10:30 PM. This prevents getting stuck in a high-intensity 60-minute game that bleeds past his deadline.
At 11:30 PM, turn off all RGB lighting on the keyboard, mouse, and PC case. This reduces the 'stadium' atmosphere of his desk and signals the brain to start melatonin production.
When moving from the PC to the bed, the phone must stay on the desk. This prevents the 'one last scroll' that keeps him awake for another two hours.
Listen to 15 minutes of a non-fiction audiobook or 'Sleep Stories' on a dedicated player (not a phone) to bridge the gap between high-stimulus gaming and sleep.

goal: Perform 10 minutes of daily mindfulness to reduce 'Panic-Related' workplace outbursts
description: Chloe is a 26-year-old retail manager in a high-volume outlet store. When the store gets busy and customers are rude, she feels a 'heat' in her chest and often snaps at her staff, which she later regrets. She wants to be a 'calm' leader but feels overwhelmed by the constant noise. Her friction point is 'Self-Judgment'—she thinks she is 'failing' at mindfulness if her mind wanders for even a second.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be on the floor for 8 hours; noisy environment; 30-minute lunch break.
baseline.physical_constraints: High blood pressure (medication-controlled).
baseline.non_negotiables: Practice must be short enough to happen in a bathroom stall or the breakroom; must be discrete.

tasks.description: Upon arriving at work, stand by her locker for 60 seconds. Close eyes and take three 'box breaths' (inhale 4, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4) before stepping onto the sales floor.
During her mid-day break, sit in a quiet corner. Perform a 5-minute 'Body Scan'—mentally checking for tension from her toes up to her jaw. This identifies the 'heat' before it becomes an outburst.
When a racing thought occurs (e.g., 'I'm bad at my job'), she must mentally say 'Thinking' and return to her breath. This addresses her friction point of self-criticism.
As she clocks out, identify exactly one thing that went well, no matter how small (e.g., 'A customer said thank you'). This rewires the brain to look for positives after a stressful shift.

goal: Read and annotate 20 pages of a technical certification manual weekly
description: Raj is a 42-year-old IT support specialist and a father of two young children. He wants to earn a Cloud Architect certification to increase his salary, but he is perpetually exhausted. He tries to read at night, but usually falls asleep by page two. His main friction point is 'passive reading'—he realizes after ten minutes that he hasn't processed anything he just read. He also struggles with 'Dad guilt' when he studies instead of playing with his kids.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Works 9 AM to 5 PM; handles school drop-offs; home environment is loud until 8:30 PM.
baseline.physical_constraints: Severe eye strain from 8 hours of ticket-handling at work.
baseline.non_negotiables: No studying during family dinner (6 PM - 7 PM); must use physical highlighters to keep hands active.

tasks.description: Read 5 pages during the 30-minute lunch break at work while sitting in his car. The isolation from his desk and family prevents distractions.
For every paragraph read, write one 'summary keyword' in the margin. This physical action forces the brain to encode the information rather than just scanning it.
During the drive home, try to explain one concept from the reading (e.g., 'What is a server?') out loud as if explaining it to his 6-year-old. This identifies gaps in his own understanding.
On Saturday morning, spend 30 minutes reviewing all annotated pages from the week and entering key terms into a digital flashcard app (Anki).

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily performing 'Glute Medius' activation to reduce runner's knee
description: Chloe is a 23-year-old marathon trainee who is currently sidelined by IT band syndrome. She is incredibly frustrated and prone to 'testing' her knee by running too early, which restarts the injury cycle. She views rehab as 'boring' compared to her 10-mile runs. Her friction point is the lack of immediate 'sweat' or feedback from PT, making her feel like the exercises aren't working.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes via bike (15 mins); works a standing job as a barista; limited floor space in a shared house.
baseline.physical_constraints: Sharp lateral knee pain when descending stairs; weak hip abductors.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a resistance loop (mini-band); no running until she can perform 20 single-leg squats pain-free.

tasks.description: Put on the resistance band while brushing teeth and perform 2 minutes of standing lateral leg lifts. This anchors the rehab to a high-frequency daily habit.
While waiting for the espresso machine to pull a shot, perform 10 isometric glute squeezes (hold for 3 seconds). This builds awareness of the muscle group during her workday.
Perform 3 sets of 15 side-steps with the resistance band. Focus on pushing the knees out to engage the hip. If the knee twinges, reduce the range of motion.
Check off the day on a physical wall calendar. Seeing the streak of 'rehab days' serves as a visual substitute for the 'miles run' metric she misses.

goal: Unsubscribe from 5 promotional email lists per day until inbox is manageable
description: Sam is a 35-year-old with ADHD who currently has 14,000 unread emails. The 'red bubble' on his phone is a constant source of low-level anxiety, but the thought of cleaning it is overwhelming. He often gets distracted by the very promotions he's trying to delete, leading to impulse shopping. His friction point is 'completionism'—he thinks he needs to read the emails before unsubscribing.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited data on his phone; uses Gmail; high-stress job that requires constant email monitoring.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but high mental clutter.
baseline.non_negotiables: No mass-delete apps (security risk); must manually evaluate lists to avoid unsubscribing from essential work alerts.

tasks.description: Search for the word 'Unsubscribe' in his inbox. This filters out personal and work emails, leaving only the promotional clutter to deal with.
Open the first 5 promotional emails, scroll to the bottom, and hit 'Unsubscribe.' Do not read the subject line or the content to avoid distraction.
After unsubscribing from those 5 lists, delete all historical emails from those specific senders. This provides an immediate visual reduction in the 'unread' count.
Check the new, slightly lower unread count. Reward the progress by closing the mail app for at least one hour to enjoy the 'quiet.'

goal: Reduce household food waste by 50% through a 'Leftover-First' policy
description: Monica is a 29-year-old remote worker who lives alone. She tends to buy fresh produce with good intentions on Mondays but ends up throwing away wilted spinach and moldy berries by Friday. She finds 'old' food unappealing and often orders delivery when she doesn't feel like cooking something 'new.' Her friction point is 'visual forgetting'—if it's in a drawer or a back shelf, it doesn't exist.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Small refrigerator; works from home but has a habit of 'working through lunch' and then snacking.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use transparent containers; no throwing away edible food until Sunday 'Clean Out' day.

tasks.description: Designate the eye-level shelf in the fridge for items expiring within 48 hours. Move wilted greens and opened jars here every morning.
Immediately upon finishing a meal, transfer leftovers into a clear glass container. Do not use opaque plastic or foil, which hide the food.
Lunch is strictly dedicated to 'Remixing' a leftover. For example, turning a roast vegetable dinner into a salad or a wrap. No 'new' cooking is allowed for lunch.
On Sunday night, look at anything that *did* have to be thrown away. Write it on a list titled 'Don't Buy This Week' to adjust shopping habits.

goal: Engage in 15 minutes of 'Unstructured' creative play (sketching/LEGO/clay) daily
description: Thomas is a 55-year-old financial auditor who is 'deeply analytical' and feels his life has become gray and rigid. He wants to lower his blood pressure and stress levels. He finds the idea of 'playing' embarrassing and hasn't done anything artistic in 30 years. His friction point is 'Self-Consciousness'—he feels that if he's not making something 'good,' it's a waste of time, which makes him freeze up.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives alone; high-pressure deadlines; habits are usually rigid and routine-based.
baseline.physical_constraints: Early symptoms of arthritis in the hands; needs medium-to-large tactile objects.
baseline.non_negotiables: No digital screens during the play; no expectation of a 'final product'; must be done in a room other than the home office.

tasks.description: Lay out a newspaper on the dining table. This visual 'messiness' signals to the brain that the area is for play, not work or formal meals.
Use the non-dominant hand to draw circles or shapes for 5 minutes. Since the result will naturally be 'bad,' it removes the pressure of perfectionism.
Spend 10 minutes manipulating a lump of clay or building with LEGO bricks without a plan. Focus on the *feeling* of the material rather than the shape.
Immediately squish the clay or disassemble the LEGO. This reinforces that the *process* was the goal, not the object. This helps bypass his auditor-brain's need for 'output.'

goal: Attend all morning lectures (before 10 AM) for a full semester
description: Tyler is a 19-year-old sophomore who 'accidentally' skips his 8:30 AM Calculus class twice a week because he stays up playing video games or scrolling until 2 AM. He feels immense guilt every time he misses a class, which lead to a cycle of 'avoidance' where he stops checking the course portal. His friction point is 'morning inertia'—he feels physically ill if he wakes up too fast and his dorm room is so cold in the winter that he refuses to leave the blankets.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a triple-occupancy dorm (cannot use loud alarms); shared bathroom is 20 feet down the hall.
baseline.physical_constraints: Extreme morning grogginess (sleep inertia); seasonal affective tendencies.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be in the classroom seat before the professor starts; no 'sleeping in' on class days even if tired.

tasks.description: Lay out a complete outfit, including shoes and a heavy hoodie, right next to the bed. This reduces the number of decisions needed in a half-awake state.
Wear a smartwatch or fitness tracker with a silent, vibrating alarm. This wakes him up through tactile stimulation without alerting his roommates.
Drink a full 16oz bottle of water stored on the nightstand immediately upon waking. This kickstarts the metabolism and helps clear 'brain fog'.
Leave the dorm 10 minutes earlier than necessary. Use the extra time to listen to an upbeat song or podcast to elevate mood before entering the lecture hall.

goal: Spend 20 minutes daily reviewing and rewriting lecture notes
description: Priya is a 21-year-old Bio-Chem major who takes messy, frantic notes during class but never looks at them again until 48 hours before an exam. She experiences high 'test anxiety' because the information feels new during finals week. Her friction point is 'perfectionism'—she feels that if her rewritten notes aren't aesthetic or color-coded like 'study-gram' accounts, they aren't useful.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Works 10 hours/week in the campus lab; heavy reading load for upper-division courses.
baseline.physical_constraints: Occasional hand cramping from long writing sessions.
baseline.non_negotiables: Review must happen on the same day as the lecture; no digital distractions (phone in another room).

tasks.description: Read through the day's notes and highlight exactly three 'Core Concepts' that are likely to be on a test. Do not worry about neatness yet.
Explain one complex concept out loud while drawing it on a small personal whiteboard. This moves the info from 'passive recognition' to 'active recall'.
Write two possible exam questions based on the day’s material in the margin of her notebook. This trains the brain to think like the professor.
Mark a 'Star' on the top of the page once the review is done. This small visual reward signals the end of the academic day.

goal: Limit 'Doomscrolling' to 30 minutes per day and zero usage during meals
description: Jordan is a freshman who feels socially isolated at a large university. He uses his phone as a 'social shield'—scrolling through TikTok during lunch in the dining hall so he doesn't look lonely. He averages 7 hours of screen time daily. His friction point is 'social anxiety'; the idea of sitting in a crowded cafeteria without a screen feels vulnerable and 'cringe'.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Requires phone for 'Duo Push' campus logins and checking class schedules.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (neck pain) from looking down at the screen.
baseline.non_negotiables: No phone usage while sitting in the dining hall; social media apps must have a time-limit lock.

tasks.description: Place the phone inside a zippered bag pocket before entering the dining hall. It cannot be touched until he has finished eating and left the building.
While eating, identify three physical details of the room (e.g., the color of someone's backpack, the music playing). This grounds him in the physical world rather than the digital one.
Set the iOS/Android app limit to 30 minutes for social media. If the limit is hit, he must do 5 minutes of 'real world' activity (e.g., tidying his desk) before overriding it if necessary.
Carry a physical book or magazine. If the urge to 'shield' himself in public arises, he must read the book instead of scrolling. This still provides a 'focus' but reduces dopamine-loop behavior.

goal: Save $500 in an emergency fund by the end of the semester (90 days)
description: Sam is a 22-year-old senior who is terrified about post-grad life. He has $0 in savings and a habit of 'stress-buying' $7 lattes and late-night pizza when school gets hard. He views money as something that 'just disappears.' His friction point is 'Ostrich Effect'—he is so scared of his bank balance that he refuses to open his banking app for weeks, leading to overdraft fees.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Income is limited to a 15-hour/week work-study job; paying for his own textbooks and groceries.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must check bank balance every Monday; no use of credit cards for 'wants' (takeout/entertainment).

tasks.description: Every time he makes coffee in his dorm instead of buying it, he must manually transfer $5 into a separate 'Emergency' savings account. This makes the saving feel 'earned'.
Log into the banking app for exactly 2 minutes. Write the current balance in a hidden note on his phone. Facing the number reduces the anxiety of the unknown.
For any non-essential purchase over $15 (e.g., clothes, tech, games), he must wait 24 hours before checking out. Often, the impulse passes by the next day.
At the end of the week, 'round down' the checking account to the nearest $10 and move the 'extra' change to savings. (e.g., if balance is $142.50, move $2.50).

goal: Drink 64oz of water and eat at least one vegetable at every dinner
description: Chloe is a 20-year-old student living in an off-campus apartment for the first time. She relies on 'box mac and cheese' and frozen nuggets because she doesn't know how to cook. She feels lethargic and her skin is breaking out. Her friction point is 'culinary intimidation'—she thinks cooking a vegetable requires complex recipes and expensive tools she doesn't have.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shared kitchen with 3 roommates; limited fridge space; small grocery budget ($50/week).
baseline.physical_constraints: Fatigue from poor nutrition.
baseline.non_negotiables: No expensive 'superfoods'; focus on frozen or canned vegetables for cost and longevity.

tasks.description: Use a 32oz reusable bottle. Mark a line at the halfway point—she must finish the first half before her 12 PM class and the full bottle before her 5 PM seminar.
Add a handful of frozen peas or broccoli directly into her pasta water or ramen while it cooks. It requires zero extra pans and zero 'chopping' skills.
Keep one bag of pre-washed spinach. No matter what she is eating, she must place a handful on her plate first. The goal is to finish the 'green' before the 'main'.
On Sundays, buy exactly three low-cost, long-lasting vegetables (e.g., a bag of carrots, a head of cabbage, and frozen spinach). Having them in the fridge removes the 'nothing to eat' excuse.

goal: Apply for 2 internships or relevant part-time roles every week
description: Alex is a 21-year-old Junior majoring in Economics. He knows his resume is 'thin' compared to his peers, but he feels paralyzed by the fear of being rejected or ignored by big firms. He spends hours 'scrolling' job boards but never hits submit because he thinks his cover letters aren't perfect. His main friction point is 'comparison trap'—seeing LinkedIn posts of friends getting offers at top banks makes him feel like he's already lost, leading to 'procrastivity' where he organizes his desktop instead of applying.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Full class load (15 credits); library student-worker shift on Tuesdays/Thursdays; limited access to professional attire.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronic tension in shoulders from stress; eye strain from dual-monitor usage.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the campus Career Center portal; no 'easy-apply' spamming—each application must have a tailored bullet point.

tasks.description: On Sunday night, ensure the master resume is updated. Save it as a PDF titled 'Alex_Lastname_Resume' so there is no friction of searching for the file during a busy weekday.
On Wednesday morning, pick one job and draft only the body of the cover letter. Do not look at the 'Submit' button. The goal is just the creative writing phase.
On Friday afternoon, proofread the draft once and hit 'Submit.' Immediately close the laptop and go for a 10-minute walk or to the gym to dissipate the post-submission anxiety.
Temporarily 'Mute' three peers on LinkedIn who trigger his comparison anxiety. This protects his mental state during the 60-day push.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Box Breathing' and grounding to manage exam-season panic
description: Laila is a 20-year-old nursing student. During midterms, she experiences physical symptoms of panic: cold hands, racing heart, and 'blanking' during exams despite knowing the material. She feels that 'resting' is a sign of weakness in a competitive program. her friction point is the 'physicality' of her stress—once her heart starts racing, she can't focus on logic, which leads to a downward spiral of worry about failing her clinicals.

baseline.fixed_constraints: 8 AM labs; shared study spaces with high noise levels; requires early morning commutes.
baseline.physical_constraints: Cold extremities (Raynaud's flare-ups) when stressed; shallow breathing patterns.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be discreet enough to perform in a quiet library or a testing center chair.

tasks.description: While sitting in the lecture hall before the professor arrives, perform 4 cycles of Box Breathing (Inhale 4, Hold 4, Exhale 4, Hold 4). This lowers the baseline heart rate.
When walking between buildings, identify 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, and 1 you can taste. This interrupts racing thoughts about the future.
Rub hands together vigorously for 10 seconds and then place them on the cheeks. Focus on the sensation of warmth. This helps combat the cold hands associated with her panic response.
Spend 3 minutes writing down every worry about the next day. Once it's on paper, close the notebook to signal to the brain that the 'worrying' is complete for now.

goal: Utilize 'Office Hours' once per week for at least one challenging subject
description: Marcus is a first-generation college freshman struggling in a large 'weed-out' Chemistry course. He is intimidated by the professor and feels his questions are 'too basic.' He spends hours on YouTube trying to learn the material but still fails the practice quizzes. His friction point is 'Imposter Syndrome'—he is convinced that if he goes to office hours, the professor will realize he 'doesn't belong' in the program.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited availability due to a part-time job at the student union; long walk between science buildings.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must prepare at least one specific question before attending; no skipping weeks even if he thinks he 'understands' the current chapter.

tasks.description: While doing homework, highlight the exact step where he gets confused. This provides a 'concrete' starting point for the meeting so he doesn't have to say 'I'm just lost.'
Right before knocking on the office door, say (silently or whispered): 'I am paying for this education and it is his job to help me.' This re-frames the power dynamic.
Attend the office hours for at least 15 minutes. If there is a crowd, just listen to other students' questions. This builds comfort with the environment without the pressure to speak immediately.
Immediately after leaving, grab a favorite coffee or snack. This builds a positive reward loop around an anxiety-inducing social interaction.

goal: Engage in 15 minutes of physical 'Skill Practice' (non-competitive) 4 days a week
description: Sophie is a 19-year-old student who was forced into sports by her parents her whole life and now hates the 'gym.' She feels out of place around 'fitness influencers' in the campus rec center. She wants to be stronger and more coordinated but lacks a goal that isn't 'weight loss.' Her friction point is 'Gym Intimidation'—the loud music and clanging weights make her want to leave immediately.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Heavy backpack load causes back stiffness; lives in a top-floor dorm with no elevator.
baseline.physical_constraints: Weak core stability; hyper-mobile joints (prone to strains).
baseline.non_negotiables: Practice must be non-competitive; no 'body-checking' in mirrors during the session.

tasks.description: Find a quiet, low-traffic area of the campus park or the back of the gym. Having 'space' reduces her social anxiety and allows for experimentation.
Spend 10 minutes practicing a 'useless' but physical skill like juggling three tennis balls or standing on one leg on a BOSU ball. This builds coordination without the 'workout' pressure.
Perform 2 sets of 10 'Glute Bridges' and 'Plank' holds to support her back from carrying heavy books. Focus on the feeling of strength, not the look of the muscle.
Record how she felt (e.g., 'Strong', 'Clumsy but fun') in a journal. Do not record calories burned or miles walked.

goal: Deep-clean one 'Zone' of the apartment every Saturday morning (30 mins)
description: Tomer is a 20-year-old living in an off-campus house with four roommates. The common areas have become a source of major stress and 'roommate resentment' because no one takes initiative. Tomer has a habit of 'rage-cleaning' for four hours once a month and then burning out. His friction point is 'associative disgust'—he hates touching other people's messes, which leads him to ignore his own clutter until it becomes overwhelming.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shared kitchen and bathroom; high foot traffic; Saturday mornings are the only time the house is quiet.
baseline.physical_constraints: Dust allergy (requires a mask for deep cleaning).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use gloves to bypass sensory 'ick' factor; 30-minute hard stop to prevent burnout.

tasks.description: On Friday night, pick one specific 5x5 area (e.g., 'the microwave and surrounding counter' or 'the bathroom sink'). Declaring the zone in advance prevents 'scope creep'.
Put on rubber gloves and a face mask before starting. This physical barrier reduces the sensory 'disgust' and helps him stay focused on the task rather than the mess.
Set a timer for 25 minutes. Listen to one specific podcast episode. Scrub, wipe, and organize the designated zone until the timer or the podcast ends.
Take a 'Before' and 'After' photo of the zone. Looking at the clean space provides an immediate dopamine hit and reinforces the 'small-win' strategy.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Recall-Based' vocabulary learning for a foreign language
description: Yuna is a 19-year-old taking Intensive Japanese. She feels 'behind' because her classmates seem to memorize kanji effortlessly while she struggles. She has a habit of 'over-reading' (staring at a list of words) which creates an illusion of competence. Her friction point is 'mental strain'—active recall is hard and makes her feel 'dumb' in the moment, causing her to switch back to passive reading.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Daily 9 AM class; 45-minute bus commute; limited quiet study time in the dorm.
baseline.physical_constraints: Occasional migraines from small-print reading.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the 'Leitner System' (physical flashcards); no apps allowed during this specific session to avoid notification-hopping.

tasks.description: On the bus, pull out 10 physical cards. Look at the English side and try to visualize the Kanji/Kana for 5 seconds before flipping. This forces the brain to struggle to find the information.
Place any cards she failed to recall into a 'Daily' box. Cards she got right go into a 'Weekly' box. This creates a visual representation of her learning progress.
For the 3 hardest words of the day, trace the strokes in the air with her finger while saying the meaning out loud. This adds a kinesthetic component to the memory.
Write down 'I missed X today, and that's how I'll remember it tomorrow.' This addresses her friction point by reframing errors as essential steps.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Social Connection' activity daily (non-digital)
description: Carlos is a 21-year-old engineering student who has spent the last two years 'hermiting' in his room. He wants to build better social skills before entering the workforce but feels 'socially rusty.' His friction point is 'Overthinking'—he scripts entire conversations in his head and then gets too anxious to actually speak. He fears being 'the weird guy' who tries too hard.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Heavy lab schedule; lives in a single-occupancy studio; spends 12+ hours a day on a PC.
baseline.physical_constraints: Social-anxiety induced sweating and stuttering.
baseline.non_negotiables: Activity must involve a real-time interaction with a human; no texting or social media comments count.

tasks.description: Make eye contact and say 'Hi' or 'Good morning' to exactly one person (bus driver, barista, or classmate) without any follow-up expected.
Identify one genuine thing to compliment someone on (e.g., 'That's a cool laptop sticker'). Deliver it and then walk away. This builds the 'approach' muscle without the 'conversation' pressure.
Ask a classmate one question about a shared experience: 'What did you think of that quiz?' or 'Are you doing the lab report tonight?' Listen to the answer for 10 seconds before replying.
After the interaction, notice if his heart rate has returned to normal. Recognize that he survived the interaction, which slowly desensitizes the panic response.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily researching and drafting a post-grad budget
description: Fatima is a senior who has no idea how much it costs to live in the city she’s moving to for her job. She feels 'financially illiterate' and avoids looking at her bank account. Her friction point is 'numerical overwhelm'—looking at rent prices and utility estimates makes her feel like she’ll never have enough money, leading to a state of 'spending paralysis' where she doesn't even buy things she needs.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Currently on a meal plan; no previous experience with bills (phone/internet); finishing her senior thesis.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a simple spreadsheet (not complex software); focus on one expense category at a time (e.g., just rent, then just food).

tasks.description: Spend 10 minutes on an apartment site looking at average prices for a 1-bedroom or shared flat in her target neighborhood. Enter the average number into her 'Draft Budget'.
Ask a friend or family member what they pay for utilities (electricity/water) in that city. This turns a scary unknown into a concrete data point.
Estimate her weekly food cost based on actual grocery store prices online versus her current habit. Add a 10% 'buffer' to the number to ease her anxiety about 'missing' a cost.
Compare the total estimated expenses to her projected entry-level salary. Identifying a gap early allows her to look for roommates now rather than panicking in six months.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Posture Correction' and core stabilization
description: Zack is a 20-year-old student who spends 10+ hours a day hunched over a laptop or his phone. He has started experiencing chronic lower back pain and 'tingling' in his hands. He feels 'too young' to have back problems and finds typical 'old person' stretches embarrassing. His friction point is 'consistency'—he does the stretches once when it hurts, then forgets for a week until the pain returns.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Long periods of sitting in non-ergonomic library chairs; carries a 20lb backpack; limited budget for a better desk chair.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tight hamstrings; forward-head posture ('tech neck').
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be doable in public/library without looking 'weird'; no equipment required.

tasks.description: Every time he finishes a class or a study session, perform 10 chin tucks. This realigns the cervical spine and reverses the 'head-forward' lean of phone usage.
While sitting in a lecture or the library, perform 20 subtle pelvic tilts to engage the deep core and prevent the lower back from 'locking up' in a slump.
Ensure both backpack straps are tight and the bag is high on the back before walking. This uses the weight of his books to pull his shoulders back rather than down.
When he returns to his dorm, stand against the door and perform 10 'Wall Angels' to open the chest. This is the only part that requires a wall and marks the end of his 'sitting' day.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying the 'Ivy Lee' method for academic prioritization
description: Maya is a 20-year-old theater major with a minor in Business. She is constantly overwhelmed by her rehearsal schedule and academic deadlines, often feeling 'busy' but ending the day realizing she hasn't touched her most important paper. Her friction point is 'multi-tasking addiction'—she tries to answer emails during rehearsals and read scripts during dinner, leading to poor performance in both. She feels a physical sense of panic when looking at a long to-do list.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Rehearsals from 6 PM to 10 PM; variable class schedule; limited access to a quiet desk.
baseline.physical_constraints: High cortisol levels; recurring tension headaches.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a physical pocket planner (not an app) to reduce digital distraction; list must be created the night before.

tasks.description: At the end of each day, write down exactly six (and only six) tasks for tomorrow in order of true importance. No 'bonus' tasks allowed.
In the morning, work on the first task exclusively. Do not look at task #2 until task #1 is either completed or the time block for it has expired.
During the first hour of study, put the phone in a bag on 'Focus Mode'. This prevents the 'switching cost' that ruins her deep work.
At the end of the day, any unfinished items move to tomorrow's list of six. Do not beat yourself up; just move the data point.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Self-Compassion' journaling after academic setbacks
description: Daniel is a 21-year-old Pre-Med student who is extremely hard on himself. If he gets anything lower than an 'A', he spirals into a week of 'worthlessness' where he stops eating well and stops socializing. His friction point is 'Perfectionistic Self-Criticism'—he believes that if he is kind to himself after a mistake, he will become 'lazy' and fail his MCATs.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a high-pressure competitive dorm; heavy lab workload; limited free time.
baseline.physical_constraints: Insomnia triggered by rumination over grades.
baseline.non_negotiables: Journaling must be private; must focus on 'common humanity' (the idea that everyone struggles).

tasks.description: When a grade or feedback is disappointing, write a paragraph to yourself as if you were writing to a friend who just had the same experience. Use the same tone of voice.
Write down one sentence acknowledging that millions of other students have felt this exact disappointment. This reduces the 'isolation' of failure.
Place a hand over the heart or on the cheek for 60 seconds while breathing slowly. This activates the parasympathetic nervous system and counteracts the 'shame' response.
Identify one small, non-academic thing he can do for his health today (e.g., eat a piece of fruit). Doing this proves he is still worthy of care despite the grade.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Professional Email Etiquette' and cold-outreach
description: Fatima is a 22-year-old first-gen student majoring in Sociology. She wants to work in policy but doesn't have any family connections in the field. She finds the language of 'professional networking' to be formal and intimidating, often over-thinking her emails for days until the opportunity passes. Her friction point is 'fear of being a burden'—she worries that she is annoying busy professionals by asking for advice.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 60 minutes each way; limited access to professional templates; high anxiety during 'social' tasks.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the campus alumni database; goal is 'informational interviews', not asking for a job directly.

tasks.description: Find one person on the alumni network who has a career she admires. Save their name and a link to their profile in a 'Networking' doc.
Using a basic template, spend 5 minutes finding one specific detail about their work to mention. This makes the email feel genuine rather than automated.
Hit 'Send' exactly at noon. Do not re-read the email more than twice. The goal is the *action* of sending, not the 'perfection' of the prose.
Record the date of the email sent. If they don't respond, she marks it as a 'Win' for the effort of reaching out. This detaches her self-worth from the response rate.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily performing 'Upper Cross Syndrome' corrective exercises
description: Zoe is a 19-year-old architecture student who spends hours hunched over a drafting table and a laptop. She has started developing 'rounded shoulders' and a persistent ache in her upper back. She feels 'old' because of her posture. Her friction point is 'Lack of Visual Feedback'—she does the stretches but doesn't see a 'straighter' back immediately, which makes her feel like the effort is futile.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Drafting studio sessions can last 4 hours; shared dorm room; budget doesn't allow for a standing desk.
baseline.physical_constraints: Weak rhomboids; tight pectorals; occasional numbness in fingertips from nerve compression.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be performed away from the desk; must use a doorway for 'chest openers'.

tasks.description: Place forearms on the doorframe and lean forward gently. Hold for 30 seconds. This physically reverses the 'hunch' from drafting.
Stand against a wall. Form a 'Y' with your arms, then pull down into a 'W', then out into a 'T'. Hold each for 10 seconds. This activates the weak back muscles.
While walking to class, focus on squeezing the shoulder blades together and 'tucking' them into the back pockets. This builds postural awareness in motion.
Wiggle fingers and check for the 'tingling' sensation. If it has decreased, she logs it. This provides the 'internal' feedback she needs to stay consistent.

goal: Spend 20 minutes daily practicing 'Critical Thinking' through diverse media analysis
description: Sam is a 20-year-old Political Science student who feels his worldviews are becoming 'echo-chambered' by his social media feed. He wants to be a better debater and writer. His friction point is 'Confirmation Bias'—he feels a physical sense of annoyance when reading opinions he disagrees with, leading him to quickly close the tab or dismiss the author as 'wrong'.

baseline.fixed_constraints: High screen time; limited budget for news subscriptions; campus environment leans heavily one way.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must read one article from a source outside his 'political bubble' daily; must identify the 'Steel Man' argument (the strongest version of the opposing view).

tasks.description: Using a neutral news aggregator (like AllSides or Ground News), find a topic of the day and read the 'Opposing' viewpoint's lead article.
Identify three 'loaded' words or adjectives used in the article to sway the reader's emotion. Write them down to de-mystify the persuasive technique.
Write two sentences summarizing the author's argument so well that the author themselves would agree with your summary. This forces deep understanding before critique.
Rate his level of 'annoyance' after reading on a scale of 1-10. The goal is to see this number decrease over 60 days as his intellectual curiosity grows.

goal: Utilize the 'Feynman Technique' to master one complex course concept daily
description: Liam is a 20-year-old Physics major who gets 'A's on homework but 'C's on exams because he relies on rote memorization rather than deep understanding. He realizes he can't explain why a formula works, only how to plug in numbers. His friction point is 'Intellectual Vanity'—he hates feeling confused and often skips the hard parts of a chapter to maintain the illusion that he's 'getting it.' He needs a way to expose his knowledge gaps without bruising his ego.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Heavy lab report workload; shared dorm with a roommate who is always present; 15-minute walk to the library.
baseline.physical_constraints: Occasional 'tech-neck' from looking down at textbooks.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a physical whiteboard or a blank sheet of paper; explanation must be verbalized out loud.

tasks.description: Write the name of one difficult concept at the top of a blank page. Choose something that felt 'fuzzy' during today's lecture.
Write an explanation of the concept as if teaching it to a 10-year-old. Avoid using technical jargon. If he hits a point where he must use a complex word, he has found a knowledge gap.
Go back to the textbook specifically to find the answer to the 'gap' identified in the previous step. Read only the relevant section to avoid getting lost in the whole chapter.
Create one simple analogy for the concept (e.g., 'Electricity is like water flowing through a pipe'). If he can create a working analogy, he truly understands the logic.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Active Social Scanning' to reduce social anxiety
description: Chloe is a 19-year-old freshman who feels 'invisible' on campus. She walks with her head down, earbuds in, and shoulders hunched to avoid eye contact. She wants to make friends but her 'resting' defensive posture makes her appear unapproachable. Her friction point is 'Mind-Reading'—she assumes that if someone looks at her, they are judging her outfit or her walk, which triggers a flush of embarrassment.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Crowded dining hall; busy quad between classes; earbuds are a 'safety' item she struggles to remove.
baseline.physical_constraints: Social anxiety-induced sweating; tight chest when in large groups.
baseline.non_negotiables: No phone usage while walking between buildings; must be physically present in a high-traffic student area.

tasks.description: Walk across the campus quad with earbuds in but volume at zero. Keep eyes at 'horizon level' rather than on the ground. This is the first step in breaking the 'hiding' habit.
Identify three people and notice their facial expressions. Observe that most people are 'neutral' or in their own world, not judging her. This counters her 'mind-reading' bias.
Make accidental eye contact with one person and hold it for exactly one second before looking away. No smile or greeting required—just the 'glance' is the victory.
Once inside a building, take three deep breaths into the belly. This signals to her nervous system that the 'threat' of being seen was managed safely.

goal: Execute a 15-minute 'Professional Online Presence' audit weekly
description: Marcus is a 21-year-old Senior in Marketing. His LinkedIn is a mess of half-finished descriptions and his Instagram has photos from freshman year that he wouldn't want a recruiter to see. He feels 'cringe' talking about his accomplishments. His friction point is 'Self-Promotion Dread'—he feels that appearing professional is 'fake' or 'try-hard,' which keeps him from polishing his brand.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited professional headshots; works a part-time retail job; high social media usage but low 'output'.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the 'Incognito' mode to see his profiles as others see them; focus on 'clarity' over 'bragging'.

tasks.description: Search his own name in an incognito browser tab. Spend 2 minutes reviewing the top 3 results. Note any 'red flag' photos or dead links.

Edit the bio of one platform (LinkedIn or Portfolio) to be more specific. Replace 'Student' with 'Marketing Student specializing in Data Analytics.' Specificity feels less like bragging and more like a fact.
Review the 'tagged' photos on Instagram. Untag himself from any that appear unprofessional (e.g., visible red solo cups or messy dorm rooms).
Endorse one peer for a skill on LinkedIn. This often triggers a 'return' endorsement, building his profile strength without him having to 'ask' for it.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Intuitive' meal planning to avoid overspending
description: Sita is a 20-year-old student who recently moved off-campus. She has no idea how to cook for one and ends up buying expensive 'convenience' meals at the campus market. She is currently $100 over her monthly budget. Her friction point is 'Ingredient Fatigue'—she buys a whole head of celery for one recipe, it rots, and she feels like she wasted money, which leads her back to frozen pizza.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Small dorm-sized fridge; only has one pot and one pan; shared kitchen is often dirty.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: No shopping without a list; must check the pantry/fridge before leaving the house.

tasks.description: Open the fridge and cabinets. Write down 3 items that are close to expiring. This makes the meal plan 'resource-based' rather than 'craving-based'.
Pick one 'Base' (e.g., rice or black beans) and brainstorm two different ways to eat it (e.g., burrito bowl today, fried rice tomorrow). This solves her 'ingredient fatigue'.
Portion out one meal-sized bag of frozen veggies. This ensures that even if she is 'too tired' to cook, she has a $1 healthy addition to a quick meal.
Look at the receipt from her last grocery trip and circle the one item she 'impulse bought'. Acknowledge it without guilt to build awareness for the next trip.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Dynamic Stretching' for better athletic mobility
description: Zack is a 19-year-old intramural basketball player who keeps getting minor 'tweak' injuries (pulled groins, stiff ankles). He thinks stretching is 'for old people' and prefers to just jump into the game. He feels 'stiff' when he wakes up. His friction point is 'Impatience'—he wants the high-intensity play and views the 'warm-up' as a boring barrier to the fun.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Games are usually late at night (9 PM); limited space in a cramped dorm for floor stretches.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tight hamstrings; limited ankle dorsiflexion from a previous sprain.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be standing-based exercises (no room to lie down); must be done *before* he leaves for the court.

tasks.description: In the dorm hallway, perform 15 forward-back and 15 side-to-side leg swings on each leg. This opens the hips and reduces the risk of groin pulls.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and rock from heels to toes 20 times. This lubricates the ankle joint that Zack usually ignores.
Perform 10 large arm circles and 10 'torso twists'. This preps the upper body for the reaching and twisting required in basketball.
After the game, note if he felt 'stiff' or 'fluid' on a 1-5 scale. Seeing the 'fluid' score increase will eventually override his impatience with the warm-up.

goal: Build a 'Professional Portfolio' with 3 completed projects by day 90
description: Javier is a 20-year-old Computer Science junior who feels like he’s 'just a student' because he has no real-world proof of his skills. He starts many side projects but abandons them as soon as they get difficult or when a new exam looms. His friction point is 'Feature Creep'—he tries to build a complex app from day one, gets overwhelmed by bugs, and quits. He also struggles with 'tutorial hell,' where he can follow a video but can't write code from scratch.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Heavy coding lab on Wednesdays; shared apartment with distracting roommates; limited server space for hosting.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but high mental fatigue from a 15-unit course load.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use GitHub to track progress; projects must be 'deployed' (visible link); no copy-pasting code from tutorials without documentation.

tasks.description: On day 1 of a new project, write down exactly three core features. Any 'bonus' ideas must be put in a 'Future' list. This prevents the project from becoming too big to finish.
Write and push at least five lines of code to GitHub every day. It could be a simple CSS fix or a new function. This maintains the 'green square' streak and momentum.
Every Friday, write the README for the current progress. Explain *why* you chose a specific technology. This prepares him for technical interviews.
Once a project hits a 'beta' stage, show the link to one classmate or professor and ask for one specific critique. Facing feedback early reduces his fear of being 'judged' later.

goal: Reduce 'Late-Night Snacking' and eat all meals within a 10-hour window
description: Mia is a 19-year-old student who lives in a dorm with a 'midnight' food culture. She often eats ramen or cereal at 1 AM while studying. She wakes up feeling bloated, nauseous, and skips her 9 AM gym class as a result. Her friction point is 'Social Pressure'—her friends gather for 'study snacks' late at night, and she feels lonely or left out if she doesn't participate in the shared junk food.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dorm kitchen is dirty; dining hall closes at 8 PM; shared microwave in the hall.
baseline.physical_constraints: Occasional acid reflux from eating right before lying down.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must start eating by 10 AM; all caloric intake must stop by 8 PM; water and herbal tea are allowed later.

tasks.description: Set a phone alarm for 7:45 PM titled 'Last Call for Food.' This gives her a 15-minute window to eat a final, satisfying protein-heavy snack before the window closes.
When the midnight snack run happens, she must bring a large mug of peppermint or ginger tea. This gives her something to 'do' with her hands while still socializing with her friends.
Move all 'trigger' foods (chips, noodles) into an opaque bin under the bed or in the back of the closet. If she can't see them, the impulse to eat them while studying is halved.
Upon waking, rate her 'Morning Energy' from 1-10. Seeing a '9' on days she didn't eat at 1 AM will eventually outweigh the temporary pleasure of the late-night ramen.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Active Recall' for an upcoming final exam
description: Ethan is a 21-year-old History major who feels like he knows the material until he sees a blank test paper. He realizes he’s just 'recognizing' facts in his notes rather than 'retrieving' them. His friction point is 'Mental Pain'—active recall is much harder than re-reading, and he often feels like he’s 'failing' during his study sessions, leading him to return to comfortable, passive highlighting.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Finals are in 3 weeks; 20+ hours of reading required; limited desk space.
baseline.physical_constraints: Lower back pain from long library sessions.
baseline.non_negotiables: No re-reading of chapters allowed during this 15-minute block; must use 'Closed-Book' methods only.

tasks.description: Take a blank sheet of paper and write down everything you remember about a specific topic (e.g., 'Causes of the French Revolution') for 5 minutes without looking at notes.
Open the textbook and use a red pen to add the details you missed to the Brain-Dump sheet. This visually highlights where your knowledge 'holes' are.
Create exactly three flashcards based *only* on the red-pen items. This ensures you are studying what you actually forgot, not what you already know.
Perform a 'standing quad stretch' for 30 seconds after the session. This relieves the back pain and associates the 'hard' mental work with physical relief.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Low-Stimulus' walking daily to reduce cortisol
description: Sarah is a 22-year-old student who feels 'constantly wired' but tired. She spends her breaks between classes scrolling through 'Rage Bait' on social media or listening to high-intensity true-crime podcasts. She has started having minor panic attacks in the library. Her friction point is 'Boredom Phobia'—she feels anxious if she isn't 'consuming' content, making a silent walk feel like a waste of time or a space for scary thoughts.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives on a noisy urban campus; limited access to true 'nature'; high academic pressure.
baseline.physical_constraints: Elevated resting heart rate; shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: No phone; no earbuds; must be outside even if the weather is 'average' (using appropriate gear).

tasks.description: Zip her phone into the deepest pocket of her backpack before leaving the building. This removes the 'reflex' to check it at a stoplight.
As she walks, she must identify 5 colors she sees and 3 non-human sounds she hears (e.g., wind, a bird, a distant siren). This keeps the mind 'busy' without the dopamine overload of a screen.
Focus on making the exhale longer than the inhale. (Inhale 3, Exhale 5). This physically forces the body out of 'Fight or Flight' and into 'Rest and Digest' mode.
Once back at her desk, record her 'Agitation Level' from 1-10. This creates a feedback loop where she can see the walk actually working over several weeks.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily learning 'Critical Personal Finance' for post-grad life
description: Wei is a 21-year-old international student who is staying in the country for a job after graduation. He has no idea how credit scores work, what a 401k is, or how to file taxes in this country. He feels 'behind' and is worried about being scammed or making a massive financial mistake. His friction point is 'Jargon Barrier'—he finds financial terms in English confusing and often gives up after reading one dense article.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited budget; no existing credit history; parents can't help as they don't know the local system.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use reputable educational sources (e.g., Investopedia or government sites); focus on one term per day.

tasks.description: Pick one 'scary' word (e.g., 'APR' or 'Escrow'). Find a simple definition and write it in his own words in a dedicated 'Finance' notebook.
Take the term of the day and apply it to a real number. If learning about 'Interest,' calculate how much $100 grows in a year at 5%. This makes the abstract concept concrete.
Identify one action needed to build credit (e.g., 'apply for a secured card'). Research the requirements for that specific action. He doesn't have to do it yet, just understand the 'how'.
Review his weekly spending. Identify one 'subscription' he forgot about. Canceling it provides an immediate 'Financial Win' that keeps him motivated.

goal: Conduct two 15-minute 'Career Networking' outreach sessions per week
description: Amir is a 21-year-old first-generation college senior majoring in Communications. He feels 'behind' because he doesn't have family connections in his industry. He experiences intense 'transactional anxiety'—the feeling that reaching out to alumni is 'using' them. His friction point is a tendency to over-edit a single LinkedIn message for three hours, only to delete it out of fear of sounding 'unprofessional' or 'annoying'.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Works 20 hours a week at the campus mailroom; senior thesis deadline approaching; uses a slow, hand-me-down laptop.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but high social anxiety manifests as sweaty palms and a racing heart when sending emails.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the University's alumni directory; no 'copy-paste' mass messages; must follow up once if no response in 10 days.

tasks.description: On Tuesday morning, identify exactly two alumni on LinkedIn who have jobs that look interesting. Bookmark their profiles and close the browser. Do not attempt to message them yet.
On Thursday, write a 3-sentence message to the identified alumni. Focus on one specific detail of their career. Set a timer for 10 minutes—once it rings, you must stop editing.
Hit 'Send' while standing up or walking. Moving physically helps Amir dissipate the adrenaline spike associated with the outreach.
Enter the date and name in a simple spreadsheet. Mark the task as 'Success' regardless of whether they reply. This shifts the focus from 'Response' to 'Effort'.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Active Rest' away from all blue light
description: Chloe is a 19-year-old Computer Science freshman who spends upwards of 12 hours a day on screens between coding labs and social media. She suffers from 'Digital Overstimulation,' which leaves her feeling physically exhausted but mentally 'vibrating.' Her friction point is 'Quiet Phobia'—the moment she puts her phone away, her brain starts replaying every awkward social interaction or upcoming deadline, leading her to grab her phone again for 'numbing'.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shared dorm room (high noise); limited access to green space; required to use a screen for almost all assignments.
baseline.physical_constraints: Persistent dry-eye syndrome; 'Tech-neck' tension at the base of the skull.
baseline.non_negotiables: No phone; no earbuds; no Kindle/tablets; must occur in a seated or lying position.

tasks.description: Place the phone in a desk drawer and physically walk away from it. Creating physical distance reduces the 'phantom vibration' urge.
Look at an object exactly 20 feet away for 20 seconds, then close eyes tightly for 1 minute to stimulate tear production for her dry-eye syndrome.
Pick one physical object in the room (e.g., a plant, a shoe, a poster) and study its textures and colors for 5 minutes. This gives the brain a 'low-dopamine' task to prevent the spiral into anxiety.
Perform 5 rounds of 'Physiological Sighing' (double inhale, long exhale). This signals the nervous system to shift out of high-alert mode before she returns to her work.

goal: Spend 20 minutes daily practicing 'Active Recall' for a high-stakes final exam
description: Toby is a 20-year-old student in a rigorous Organic Chemistry course. He spends hours 're-reading' his notes and thinks he's prepared, but consistently bombs his practice tests. He realizes he has fallen into the 'Fluency Illusion'—the material looks familiar, but he can't produce it from scratch. His friction point is 'Mental Fatigue'—active recall is much harder than reading, and he feels 'stupid' when he can't remember a reaction, causing him to quit early.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Heavy workload from other classes; limited library hours; requires a high-quality internet connection for molecular modeling software.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but high levels of stress-induced fatigue.
baseline.non_negotiables: No looking at the textbook during the recall session; must use a physical whiteboard or a blank paper.

tasks.description: Write down the name of one reaction or mechanism. Spend 5 minutes drawing the structures and arrows from memory. Do not check the book, even if you get stuck.
Open the textbook and use a different colored pen to fill in the missing steps or corrected arrows. This visually 'shocks' the brain into remembering the missing data next time.
Verbally explain the corrected mechanism out loud to an empty room (or a rubber duck). If he can't explain it simply, he doesn't understand it yet.
Create exactly one physical flashcard based on the mistake made today. This ensures he is building a deck of his actual weaknesses, not general info.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily on 'Life Skills' education (taxes, credit, or basic repairs)
description: Jordan is a 22-year-old senior about to move into his first solo apartment. He feels a deep sense of 'adulting incompetence'—he doesn't know how to read a lease, fix a leaky faucet, or how taxes are actually filed. His friction point is 'Ego Threat'—he is embarrassed to ask his peers for help and finds the information online either too complex or patronizing, leading to total avoidance of 'real world' preparation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited budget; no access to a car for 'hands-on' classes; shared apartment with roommates who are equally clueless.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use reputable 'How-To' sources (e.g., government sites or trade-verified videos); no TikTok-only 'hacks' allowed.

tasks.description: Pick one 'adult' term (e.g., 'Security Deposit', 'Deductible', 'APR'). Find a definition and write it in his own words. This demystifies the language that keeps him intimidated.
Watch one 10-minute video on a basic home repair (e.g., 'How to unclog a drain' or 'How to find a breaker box'). Write down the three steps involved. He doesn't need the tools yet, just the logic.
Search for a sample 'Apartment Lease' online and read the first two pages. Highlight the sections regarding 'late fees' or 'utilities.' This makes the upcoming move feel less like a gamble.
Text a parent or older mentor one specific question about their household setup (e.g., 'What kind of renters insurance do you have?'). This breaks the 'pride' barrier in small, manageable doses.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily performing 'Lower-Limb' mobility and foot-strengthening drills
description: Zoe is a 20-year-old student who recently started jogging but keeps getting 'Shin Splints.' She has 'flat feet' and a habit of ignoring pain until she can't walk. She wants to be fit but finds the 'Gym' environment too high-pressure and loud. Her friction point is 'Impatience'—she wants the 'Runner's High' immediately and views 10 minutes of mobility as 'not real exercise,' leading her to skip it and re-injure herself.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor walk-up; limited floor space in her dorm; shared bathroom makes it hard to 'ice' regularly.
baseline.physical_constraints: Weak arches (overpronation); recurring shin pain; tight hip flexors from long study sessions.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be standing or chair-based (no floor room); must be performed before putting on running shoes.

tasks.description: While sitting at her desk, place a small hand towel under her bare foot. Use her toes to scrunch the towel toward her for 3 minutes per foot. This targets the intrinsic muscles to support her flat arches.
While studying, straighten one leg under the desk and hold for 10 seconds, then switch. Repeat 10 times. This engages the quads without requiring her to leave her study 'flow'.
Perform slow, controlled calf raises while brushing teeth. Focus on keeping the weight on the big toe to prevent the ankle from rolling outward.
Log her shin pain on a 1-10 scale after her walk/run. If the pain is higher than a 3, she must skip the next run and double the 'Towel-Scrum' duration.

goal: Prepare and consume a 30g protein breakfast daily within 1 hour of waking
description: Liam is a 21-year-old student athlete who 'crashes' during his 2 PM economics lecture. He usually skips breakfast or just has a sugary energy drink on the way to his 9 AM class. He finds 'healthy cooking' expensive and time-consuming. His friction point is 'Morning Brain-Fog'—he is too tired to make decisions in the kitchen, so he defaults to the easiest (and least nutritious) option.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited dorm fridge space; shared microwave is often occupied; 15-minute walk to the nearest lecture hall.
baseline.physical_constraints: High metabolism; prone to 'hangry' outbursts if blood sugar drops.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be under $3 per serving; must be portable enough to eat while walking to class.

tasks.description: Portion out protein powder or hard-boil 2 eggs before bed. This removes the 'decision' element from his groggy morning self.
Mix instant oats with a protein shake instead of water. This creates a high-protein, slow-burning fuel source that can be made in a travel mug.
Drink 8oz of water while the oats are 'setting'. This wakes up the digestive system and reduces the 'fog' before he leaves the room.
Set a phone notification for 2:15 PM to rate his alertness. Noticing that he didn't crash will validate the morning effort.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying 'Deep Work' techniques to the hardest course assignment
description: Anaya is a 20-year-old English major who is a chronic 'multi-tasker'. She writes her essays with 12 Chrome tabs open, music playing, and her phone buzzing. She feels like she's 'working' for 5 hours but only produces one paragraph. Her friction point is 'Digital FOMO'—the physical anxiety she feels when she isn't reachable via group chat.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Heavy workload of 100+ pages of reading per week; noisy library environment; feels pressured to respond to group projects instantly.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Phone must be in a different room or a locked bag; all social media tabs must be closed.

tasks.description: Send a quick message to her project groups: 'Focusing for 30 mins, back soon.' This eliminates the guilt and anxiety of being 'offline'.
Open only the word processor. If she needs to look up a quote, she must write [CITATE LATER] and keep writing. This avoids the 'Wikipedia Rabbit Hole'.
If she gets stuck, she must move to a physical notepad for 2 minutes to sketch out the idea before returning to the keyboard.
Record the word count produced in just that 15-minute block. Realizing she did more in 15 mins of 'Deep Work' than 2 hours of 'Multi-tasking' will break the habit.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Reflective Journaling' to improve social intelligence
description: Marcus is a 22-year-old engineering student who feels like he 'puts his foot in his mouth' during team projects. He is technically gifted but often misses social cues, leading to awkward silences or unintended offense. His friction point is 'Defensiveness'—when a social interaction goes poorly, he blames the other person rather than reflecting on his own communication style.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a high-pressure studio environment; works late-night shifts at the library; limited privacy.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Journal must be digital (password protected) for total privacy; focus on one interaction per day.

tasks.description: Write down exactly what was said during one brief interaction today. Avoid using emotional language; just record the dialogue as if it were a script.
Think of one alternative way he could have phrased his part of the conversation. How would that have changed the other person's body language?
Write one sentence describing what the other person might have been feeling (e.g., 'They might have been stressed about their own deadline'). This breaks his habit of blaming.
Write down one 'Social Goal' for tomorrow (e.g., 'I will ask one clarifying question before giving my opinion').

goal: Spend 20 minutes daily learning 'Cloud Fundamentals' via free online labs
description: Sasha is a 19-year-old IT student who feels the curriculum is 'too theoretical.' She wants to have hands-on experience with AWS or Azure but is terrified of accidentally running up a huge bill on her credit card. Her friction point is 'Technical Intimidation'—she feels like if she doesn't understand the whole cloud architecture immediately, she shouldn't even start.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited laptop processing power; shared Wi-Fi that can be spotty in the evenings; no budget for paid courses.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use 'Free Tier' accounts only; must set a billing alarm at $0.01 as the very first task.

tasks.description: Log into the cloud console and navigate to the 'Billing' dashboard. Set an email alert for any spending over $0.01. This eliminates her primary friction point (fear of cost).
Pick exactly one service (e.g., S3 or EC2). Read the first page of the 'Quick Start' guide and locate that service in the console. Do not launch anything yet.
Practice launching a 'Micro' instance and then immediately terminating it. Knowing exactly how to 'Turn it Off' gives her the confidence to explore further tomorrow.
Write one cloud term learned today (e.g., 'Bucket', 'Instance', 'Region') on a sticky note and put it on her monitor. This builds her technical vocabulary over time.
