She is scared of 'real' exercise because she doesn't want to make the pain worse. She feels 'clunky' and uncoordinated. Her friction point is 'Fear-Avoidance'—whenever her back feels slightly stiff, she stops moving entirely, which actually increases the stiffness.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor dorm with no elevator; limited floor space; uses a heavy backpack.
baseline.physical_constraints: Anterior pelvic tilt; limited hip mobility; tight hip flexors.
baseline.non_negotiables: No jumping; no heavy lifting; must use a yoga mat or a soft rug.

tasks.description: Perform 10 slow Cat-Cow cycles on the floor. Focus on the breath and moving one vertebra at a time to reduce tension.
Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining a flat back (5 reps per side). This builds the 'internal corset' that supports her spine.
Perform a gentle lunge stretch for 30 seconds per side. This 'unlocks' the hips that have been shortened by a day of sitting.
Write down if the movement felt like 'Good Discomfort' (stretching) or 'Bad Pain' (sharp). Recognizing the difference helps her overcome her 'fear-avoidance'.

goal: Spend 10 minutes every morning performing 'Posture Reset' and lower-back stabilization
description: Zack is a 19-year-old student who spends 8 hours a day in non-ergonomic library chairs. He wakes up with a 'stiff' lower back and 'hunched' shoulders. He often 'snaps' at classmates because he’s in low-level pain all morning. His friction point is 'Gym Intimidation'—he thinks the only way to get fit is the campus gym, which he finds too loud and public, so he stays in pain.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor walk-up; limited floor space; uses a heavy 20lb backpack; high noise dorm.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (forward head posture); tight scalene muscles; shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be standing (no floor space); must be done before putting on the backpack; no equipment needed.

tasks.description: Stand against the back of the door and perform 15 chin tucks (creating a 'double chin'). This realigns the cervical spine after a night of poor pillow support.
Form a 'W' with the arms and squeeze the shoulder blades together for 10 seconds. Repeat 5 times. This opens the chest that has been 'collapsed' by drafting or typing.
Perform 4 rounds of Box Breathing (Inhale 4, Hold 4, Exhale 4, Hold 4) while standing. This physically lowers his stress baseline before he enters the quad.
Tighten both backpack straps so the bag sits high on the back. This uses the weight of his books to pull his shoulders back rather than down.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily learning to utilize 'Reference Management' software for thesis research
description: Mateo is a 21-year-old senior majoring in Sociology. He has over 40 PDFs scattered across his 'Downloads' folder with names like 'paper_final_v2.pdf.' He is terrified of accidentally plagiarizing or losing a key source before his capstone is due. His friction point is 'Technical Overwhelm'—he tried downloading Zotero once but got frustrated by the interface and went back to manually typing citations in Word. He feels like he's 'too deep' into his current mess to fix it.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must submit a 25-page thesis by the end of the semester; shares a laptop with a younger sibling; library access is restricted to 3 nights a week.
baseline.physical_constraints: Severe eye strain from digital reading; needs a high-contrast monitor setting.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the university-approved citation style (ASA); all files must be backed up to a cloud drive daily.

tasks.description: Pick exactly 3 PDFs from the 'Downloads' folder. Rename them by 'Author-Year-Topic' and drag them into the reference manager. This makes the backlog feel like a series of 5-minute tickets rather than a mountain.
Verify that the 'Journal Title' and 'DOI' are correctly filled in for those 3 papers. Fixing these now prevents the 'Formatting Nightmare' during finals week.
Find one new article for his research and use the browser connector/plugin to save it with one click. This proves to his brain that the software is faster than his manual method.
Physically check that the green 'sync' icon is active. This ritualistic check reduces his 'loss-aversion' anxiety about his data disappearing.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Low-Stimulus' outdoor walking immediately after morning lectures
description: Hana is a 19-year-old freshman who feels 'physically buzzed' after her 9 AM Psych lecture. She immediately checks her phone to see what she missed, but the flood of social notifications makes her feel even more fragmented. She struggles with 'Attention Residue'—she's still thinking about the lecture while trying to eat lunch. Her friction point is 'Boredom Phobia'—the idea of walking without music or a podcast feels like 'dead time' she should be using to catch up.

baseline.fixed_constraints: 15-minute gap between classes; high-traffic urban campus; shared dining hall for lunch.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but experiences 'Digital Eye Strain' (burning eyes) from laptop use during class.
baseline.non_negotiables: Phone must stay in the backpack (not the pocket); no earbuds allowed; must be outside even if it's drizzling.

tasks.description: Zip the phone into the deepest pocket of the bag before leaving the lecture hall. This physical barrier stops the 'phantom reach' reflex.
Walk toward the dining hall but keep eyes on the horizon or the trees. This 'Optic Flow' naturally calms the amygdala and resets the brain's focus after intense screen time.
Identify three sounds that aren't human voices (e.g., wind, a car tire, a bird). This shifts the brain from 'Internal Rumination' to 'External Observation.'
Squeeze eyes shut for 5 seconds and then blink rapidly 10 times. This lubricates the eyes after a 60-minute 'no-blink' lecture stare.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Scenario-Based' public speaking for seminar participation
description: Wei is a 20-year-old international student who is brilliant in his written work but silent in seminars. He is terrified of making a grammatical mistake or being misunderstood. He wants to be a teaching assistant next year, but his 'Participation' grade is currently a D. His friction point is 'The Spotlight Effect'—he is convinced the entire class is waiting for him to trip over a word so they can judge his English skills.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Seminars are 3 times a week; lives in a quad-dorm with high noise; limited access to a private recording space.
baseline.physical_constraints: Social-anxiety induced dry mouth; shaky hands when holding notes.
baseline.non_negotiables: Practice must be verbal (no just thinking the words); must use a mirror or a phone camera for visual feedback.

tasks.description: Deliver one 'Opening Sentence' (e.g., 'I agree with Sarah's point, but I also think...') while looking into his own eyes in the mirror. Normalizing the sound of his own voice in English is the goal.
Practice speaking while holding a pen or a notebook firmly. This gives the 'nervous energy' in his hands a physical outlet that isn't visible fidgeting.
Intentionally mispronounce a word, then say 'Sorry, let me rephrase that,' and continue. This 'Fail-Safe' practice proves to his brain that a mistake isn't an academic catastrophe.
Record 30 seconds of speech on his phone. Listen to it once. He will notice that his 'accent' is far less distracting to the listener than he perceives it to be.

goal: Log and evaluate 5 minutes of 'Impulse Spending' every morning to manage the student budget
description: Jordan is a freshman who 'over-taps' his Apple Pay for late-night pizza and energy drinks. He has $0 in his 'Fun' budget by Wednesday every week. He feels a sense of 'Financial Shame' and avoids his banking app like it's a horror movie. His friction point is 'Mindless Consumption'—he doesn't view a $4 latte as 'real money' until he sees the overdraft notification.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Allowance is $60/week; shared meal plan; high pressure to 'go out' on Thursday nights.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must open the banking app before 9 AM; no 'Buy Now Pay Later' apps allowed on the phone.

tasks.description: Open the banking app and stare at the current balance for 30 seconds. No judgment allowed—just see the number. This desensitizes the fear response through repeated exposure.
Identify one purchase from yesterday. Ask: 'Did this make my day better for more than 10 minutes?' If the answer is no, mark it as a 'ghost expense.'
Calculate the 'Remaining Daily Budget' (Total Balance / Days left in the month). Write this number on a sticky note and put it on the back of his phone. This is his 'Reality Check.'
If he identifies a 'ghost expense' from yesterday, he must manually move $1 from checking to savings as a 'symbolic refund' to himself.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Lower-Body Mobility' to fix 'Lab-Stiffness'
description: Sasha is a 20-year-old Chemistry major who spends 4-hour blocks standing on hard concrete lab floors. She wakes up with 'heavy' legs and throbbing heels. She thinks 'mobility' is only for yoga influencers and feels out of place in the campus gym. Her friction point is 'Physical Exhaustion'—at the end of the day, she's so tired that the thought of 'moving more' feels like a punishment.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must wear closed-toe, non-slip shoes (heavy); limited floor space in her dorm; shared bathroom is far away.
baseline.physical_constraints: Plantar fasciitis (heel pain); tight calves; limited ankle range of motion.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be standing or chair-based (no floor room); must happen *before* she puts on her work boots.

tasks.description: While sitting at her desk, roll her bare foot over a tennis ball for 2 minutes per side. This breaks up the tension in the fascia caused by 4 hours of standing.
Slowly rock from heels to toes 20 times while holding the edge of her desk for balance. This 'pumps' blood back up from the legs and lubricates the ankle joint.
Perform one 30-second calf stretch per leg against the doorframe. This targets the root of her 'heavy leg' feeling.
Rate her leg 'heaviness' (1-10). Noticing a '3' on days she stretches versus a '7' on days she doesn't will override her 'tired' brain's desire to skip.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily learning to navigate and troubleshoot Linux terminal commands
description: Arjun is a 19-year-old CS student who feels like a 'poser' because he relies entirely on the GUI (Graphical User Interface) for his coding projects. He sees his peers using the terminal with lightning speed and feels intimidated. His friction point is 'Syntax Terror'—the fear that one wrong command will delete his entire operating system. He also deals with a noisy dorm environment that makes it hard to focus on dense documentation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Uses a budget laptop with limited RAM; must participate in a 3-hour lab session every Tuesday; shared Wi-Fi cuts out frequently.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but experiences significant 'wrist-locking' when typing under stress.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a virtual machine or a safe 'sandbox' environment for practice; no using 'sudo' commands without looking up the manual first.

tasks.description: Boot up the Linux sandbox environment and open the terminal. Type 'pwd' and 'ls' to visualize exactly where he is in the file system. This builds a mental map of the 'hidden' computer.
Pick one common command (e.g., 'grep' or 'chmod'). Type 'man [command]' and read only the first two paragraphs. Write down in plain English what the command actually does.
Intentionally mistype a command to trigger an error message. Read the error out loud. This desensitizes him to the 'red text' and trains him to see errors as data, not failures.
Perform one full file operation (create a folder, move a file, delete it) using only the keyboard. This builds the muscle memory of the command-line interface.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Monotasking' deep work on the most difficult course reading
description: Zoe is a 20-year-old Philosophy major who feels her 'brain is fried' by social media algorithms. She can't read three paragraphs of Kant without her hand reflexively reaching for her phone. Her friction point is 'Silent Anxiety'—the quietness of deep reading makes her feel like she’s missing something important online, causing a literal 'itch' to check her notifications.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must read 150 pages per week; lives in a triple-dorm; library 'Quiet Zone' is always full.
baseline.physical_constraints: Severe digital eye-strain; neck tension from looking down at her phone.
baseline.non_negotiables: Phone must be in a 'timed lock-box' or a different room; no music with lyrics allowed; must use a physical book/printed paper.

tasks.description: Place the phone in a bag, zip it, and put the bag in the closet. The 10 seconds of physical distance reduces the 'phantom vibration' effect.
Use a pencil as a physical guide while reading. Moving the tip under the words forces the eyes to stay on the line and prevents 'skipping' or 'scanning' behavior.
Keep a small notepad next to the book. If a random thought pops up (e.g., 'Check my email'), write it down and immediately return to the text. This 'exports' the anxiety to paper.
After the 10-minute timer, stand up and reach for the ceiling. This physical release rewards the brain for the 'unnatural' act of prolonged focus.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily learning to create 'Professional Data Visualizations' in Excel
description: David is a 22-year-old Business senior who knows how to enter data but makes 'ugly' charts with default colors and confusing labels. He’s noticed that his internship applications get rejected after the technical assessment. His friction point is 'Visual Overwhelm'—he finds the 'Format' menu in Excel so dense that he gets frustrated and settles for the first chart that pops up.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited laptop screen size; uses a pirated or student version of Excel; no background in design or statistics.
baseline.physical_constraints: None.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use 'real-world' datasets (e.g., from Kaggle or local news); no using the 'Recommended Charts' button—must build from scratch.

tasks.description: Take an existing chart and remove every non-essential element: gridlines, borders, and bolded axis text. This 'minimalist' practice teaches him what actually matters in data storytelling.
Select a dataset and use only shades of gray for 90% of the chart, using one single 'Highlight Color' (like blue) for the most important data point. This builds his 'Design Eye'.

Change the chart title from a boring label (e.g., 'Q3 Sales') to a descriptive headline (e.g., 'Q3 Sales Rose by 12% due to New Marketing'). This shifts his mindset from 'Data Entry' to 'Communication'.
Export the chart as a PDF and view it on a phone. If he can't understand the main point in 5 seconds, he must identify one element to simplify for tomorrow.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Scenario-Based' boundary setting with roommates
description: Maya is a 19-year-old freshman who is a chronic 'People Pleaser.' Her roommates leave dishes in the sink for days and play music at 1 AM, but Maya says nothing because she is terrified of being 'the mean one.' She feels constant low-level resentment and can't sleep. Her friction point is 'Conflict Avoidance'—she views any boundary as a battle that will end with her being kicked out of the social group.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Small shared apartment; high-stress finals week; no other housing options.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronic sleep deprivation (averaging 5 hours); experiences 'lump in throat' when trying to speak up.
baseline.non_negotiables: Practice must be verbalized (whispered or out loud); must use 'I' statements only; no 'venting' to parents instead of talking to roommates.

tasks.description: In the shower or while walking, whisper the exact phrase: 'I’m finding it hard to sleep with the music on at 1 AM; can we use headphones after midnight?' Normalizing the words reduces the physical 'lump in the throat'.
Look in the mirror and practice saying the boundary with a neutral, calm face. This proves to herself that she isn't 'being mean' or 'angry'—she's just communicating a need.
Make one very small, non-critical request (e.g., 'Could you pass the remote?' or 'Mind if I turn the light off?'). This builds the 'Asking Muscle' in a low-stakes environment.
At the end of the day, note if her resentment felt lower because she at least *prepared* to speak. This internal validation is key for long-term behavior change.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily performing 'Pelvic-Tilt' and core stability drills
description: Tomer is a 20-year-old student who recently started going to the campus gym but has developed lower-back 'twinges' after using the treadmill. He has an 'Anterior Pelvic Tilt' from years of gaming and sitting in lectures. He wants to be fit, but the pain makes him want to quit. His friction point is 'Gym Intimidation'—he feels too embarrassed to do 'rehab' exercises in front of the 'lifters' at the rec center.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dorm floor is cold/hard tile; no gym equipment in the room; limited privacy.
baseline.physical_constraints: Visible arch in lower back when standing; tight hip flexors; weak glutes.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be standing or bed-based (no floor contact needed); no equipment required; must happen before he leaves the dorm.

tasks.description: While sitting on the edge of the bed, slowly tilt the pelvis back and forth. This 'wakes up' the core muscles and helps him find a 'neutral' spine before his day starts.
While brushing teeth, perform 20 isometric glute squeezes. This counteracts the pelvic tilt and provides 'invisible' training for the muscles that protect his lower back.
Perform a 45-second wall sit against the door while checking the weather or a news app. This builds the quad and core strength needed for his gym sessions later.
Record if the back felt 'tight' or 'fluid' during his walk to class. Seeing a 'fluid' rating on a Tuesday will motivate him to repeat the tilts on Wednesday.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying 'Spaced Repetition' to active recall flashcards
description: Ben is a 19-year-old pre-law student drowning in Latin terminology and case law. He has a habit of 'cramming'—studying for 6 hours straight the night before a quiz—which leads to high stress and zero long-term retention. His friction point is 'Mental Friction'; he finds the initial struggle of remembering a card frustrating and often 'cheats' by looking at the answer too early. He also feels that 15 minutes is 'too short' to be productive, leading him to skip the habit entirely.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 30 minutes by bus; limited desk space in a quad-dorm; constant noise from roommates.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but prone to digital eye strain from excessive laptop use.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a dedicated Spaced Repetition System (SRS) like Anki or Quizlet; cards must be reviewed before 11 AM while his brain is fresh.

tasks.description: While waiting for the campus shuttle, open the app and download the day's 'due' cards. This ensures the material is ready the moment he sits down.
When a card appears, he must close his eyes and try to recall the answer for exactly 5 seconds before flipping. This 'desirable difficulty' is what builds the long-term memory.
Any card that takes more than two attempts to remember must be 'flagged.' He will spend the last 2 minutes of the session specifically re-reading the textbook context for just those flagged cards.
Take a screenshot of the 'No more cards due' screen and send it to a study buddy. This external accountability makes the 15-minute habit feel like a finished 'mission'.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Morning Mental Unloading' via a brain-dump journal
description: Lydia is a 21-year-old senior who wakes up with a 'racing mind.' She immediately starts worrying about her thesis, her graduation dress, and her student loan balance. This 'morning static' makes her feel paralyzed, often leading her to stay in bed for an hour. Her friction point is 'Self-Censorship'—she tries to write 'pretty' or 'meaningful' journal entries, which makes the process feel like another graded assignment.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Morning yoga class at 7 AM; shares a room with a light sleeper; limited privacy.
baseline.physical_constraints: Elevated morning heart rate (anxiety-linked); shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a physical notebook; handwriting can be illegible; no one else is allowed to read it.

tasks.description: Set a timer for 60 seconds and write down every single 'to-do' or 'worry' currently in the head. No sentences required—just a list of fragments to clear the mental buffer.
Write one brutally honest sentence about how she feels right now (e.g., 'I am terrified of failing'). Naming the emotion reduces its power over her day.
Identify three actionable items from the 'Messy Minute' and move them to a sticky note. The rest of the list is mentally 'discarded' for the morning.
Physically close the notebook and place it in a drawer. This physical action signals to the brain that the 'worrying phase' is over and the 'action phase' has begun.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Professional Body Language' for job interviews
description: Kevin is a 22-year-old engineering senior who is brilliant on paper but 'collapses' in person. He mumbles, hunches his shoulders, and fidgets with his pen, making him appear nervous and unreliable to recruiters. He wants to project 'Executive Presence' but feels like a 'fraud' when he tries to sit up straight. His friction point is 'Physical Discomfort'—his back muscles are weak from years of gaming, making good posture literally tiring.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Interviews are usually on Zoom; limited professional attire; shared bedroom with no full-length mirror.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (forward head posture); tight hamstrings; weak core.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the laptop camera for self-review; sessions must be standing or seated-active (no lying down).

tasks.description: Stand against a wall with heels, glutes, and head touching. Perform 10 'Wall Angels' (sliding arms up and down) to open the chest and realign the spine.
Open the webcam. Practice sitting in the 'Power Box' (hands visible but not frantic, head in the upper third of the frame). Speak one paragraph of his 'About Me' intro.
While speaking on camera, keep hands resting flat on the desk or loosely clasped. If he feels the urge to fidget, he must squeeze his toes instead (invisible to the camera).
Practice looking directly into the camera lens (not the screen) and giving a brief, 2-second smile. This builds 'digital warmth' and trust with the interviewer.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Low-Impact' morning movement to manage back spasms
description: Zoe is a 20-year-old student who recently started having lower back 'spasms' from sitting 12 hours a day at a drafting table. She is scared of 'real' exercise because she doesn't want to make the pain worse. She feels 'clunky' and uncoordinated. Her friction point is 'Fear-Avoidance'—whenever her back feels slightly stiff, she stops moving entirely, which actually increases the stiffness.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor dorm with no elevator; limited floor space; uses a heavy backpack.
baseline.physical_constraints: Anterior pelvic tilt; limited hip mobility; tight hip flexors.
baseline.non_negotiables: No jumping; no heavy lifting; must use a yoga mat or a soft rug.

tasks.description: Perform 10 slow Cat-Cow cycles on the floor. Focus on the breath and moving one vertebra at a time to reduce the 'spasm' tension.
Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining a flat back (5 reps per side). This builds the 'internal corset' that supports her spine.
Perform a gentle lunge stretch for 30 seconds per side. This 'unlocks' the hips that have been shortened by a day of sitting.
Write down if the movement felt like 'Good Discomfort' (stretching) or 'Bad Pain' (sharp). Recognizing the difference helps her overcome her 'fear-avoidance'.

goal: Spend 10 minutes every morning performing 'Posture Reset' and lower-back stabilization
description: Zack is a 19-year-old student who spends 8 hours a day in non-ergonomic library chairs. He wakes up with a 'stiff' lower back and 'hunched' shoulders. He often 'snaps' at classmates because he’s in low-level pain all morning. His friction point is 'Gym Intimidation'—he thinks the only way to get fit is the campus gym, which he finds too loud and public, so he stays in pain.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor walk-up; limited floor space; uses a heavy 20lb backpack; high noise dorm.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (forward head posture); tight scalene muscles; shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be standing (no floor space); must be done before putting on the backpack; no equipment needed.

tasks.description: Stand against the back of the door and perform 15 chin tucks (creating a 'double chin'). This realigns the cervical spine after a night of poor pillow support.
Form a 'W' with the arms and squeeze the shoulder blades together for 10 seconds. Repeat 5 times. This opens the chest that has been 'collapsed' by drafting or typing.
Perform 4 rounds of Box Breathing (Inhale 4, Hold 4, Exhale 4, Hold 4) while standing. This physically lowers his stress baseline before he enters the quad.
Tighten both backpack straps so the bag sits high on the back. This uses the weight of his books to pull his shoulders back rather than down.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying the 'Feynman Technique' to one challenging course concept
description: Liam is a 20-year-old Physics major who gets high marks on math problems but struggles with conceptual theory. He often realizes during exams that he has memorized formulas without understanding the 'why.' His friction point is 'The Illusion of Competence'—he feels smart when reading the textbook but realizes he can't explain the logic to anyone else, leading to a fear of being 'found out' by professors.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a loud fraternity house; limited whiteboard access; heavy laboratory schedule.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but experiences 'brain-lock' when under pressure.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must verbalize the explanation out loud (no silent reading); must simplify the concept for a 12-year-old level; no looking at the textbook during the 15-minute explanation phase.

tasks.description: Write the name of a complex concept (e.g., 'Quantum Entanglement') at the top of a blank sheet of paper. This defines the target for the session.
Explain the concept out loud while writing it down as if teaching a child. Avoid using technical jargon. When he hits a term he can't explain simply, he must circle it as a 'knowledge gap.'
Go back to the textbook specifically to fix the 'circled gaps' from the previous step. Read only enough to clarify that specific point.
Create one simple, real-world analogy for the concept. If he can create a working analogy, he has achieved deep understanding.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily on 'Morning Digital Satiety' using only long-form educational content
description: Chloe is a 19-year-old freshman who wakes up with 'Notification Anxiety.' She spends her first 45 minutes in bed scrolling short-form videos, which results in a fragmented attention span and high irritability. Her friction point is 'Boredom Intolerance'—without the rapid-fire dopamine of scrolling, she feels a sense of profound emptiness, which she tries to fill with more scrolling.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dorm Wi-Fi is strongest in the morning; shared room (must use headphones); high academic pressure.
baseline.physical_constraints: Eyestrain from high-brightness screens in the dark; shallow 'anxiety' breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: No TikTok, Reels, or Shorts; phone must be in 'Do Not Disturb' mode; video must be at least 15 minutes long.

tasks.description: The night before, select one 15-minute educational video (e.g., a documentary clip) and have the tab ready. This prevents the 'search loop' that leads back to social media.
Sit up at the desk to watch the video; no watching in bed. The change in posture signals to the brain that this is 'active learning,' not 'passive numbing.'
Write down exactly one interesting thing learned from the video on a sticky note. This 'output' validates the time spent as productive.
Notice the urge to scroll. Instead of acting on it, take three deep breaths. This 'urge surfing' builds the muscle of self-control.

goal: Apply for 2 internships or relevant part-time roles every week
description: Alex is a 21-year-old Senior who feels his resume is 'thin.' He knows he should be applying, but he gets stuck in 'Cover Letter Perfectionism.' He spends hours 'scrolling' job boards but never hits submit because he thinks his experience isn't enough. His friction point is 'Comparison Anxiety'—seeing his peers get offers on LinkedIn makes him feel like he's already lost, leading to avoidance.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Full class load; works 12 hours/week at the student union; limited professional wardrobe.
baseline.physical_constraints: Shoulder tension from stress.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the campus Career Center portal; no 'easy-apply' spamming—each application must have a tailored bullet point.

tasks.description: On Sunday night, ensure the master resume is updated. Save it as a PDF titled 'Alex_Resume' so there is no friction of searching for the file during a busy weekday.
On Wednesday, pick one job and draft only the body of the cover letter. Do not look at the 'Submit' button. The goal is just the creative phase.
On Friday, proofread the draft once and hit 'Submit.' Immediately close the laptop and go for a 10-minute walk to dissipate the post-submission anxiety.
Temporarily 'Mute' three peers on LinkedIn who trigger his comparison anxiety. This protects his mental state during the application push.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Scenario-Based' verbal professional pitches
description: Javier is a 21-year-old student who mumbles during career fairs. He has a great GPA but lacks 'Professional Fluency'—he struggles to explain his technical projects in a way that sounds confident. His friction point is 'Self-Consciousness'—he feels 'fake' when he speaks about his achievements, leading him to downplay his role.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Interviews coming up; lives in a triple-dorm (no privacy); high caffeine intake.
baseline.physical_constraints: Stutters slightly when nervous.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must record the audio of the pitch; must include one 'Value Statement'; no reading from a script.

tasks.description: While walking to class, whisper his 30-second 'Elevator Pitch.' This builds the muscle memory of the words without the pressure of an audience.
Record a 60-second explanation of one project. He must listen to it once. Hearing his own voice helps him identify 'filler words' like 'um' or 'like'.
Look at himself in the mirror and deliver the final sentence of his pitch. He must maintain eye contact for 3 seconds *after* he finishes speaking.
Write down: 'Facts are not bragging.' This addresses his friction point by reminding him that stating his contributions is a professional requirement.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily on 'Zero-Inbox' administrative clearance for school emails
description: Tomer is a freshman who currently has 1,200 unread emails. He has missed two assignment deadlines because the instructions were buried in his inbox. He feels 'Financial Shame' every time he sees a bank notification, so he avoids the whole app. His friction point is 'Task Avoidance'—he views a simple email as a 2-hour ordeal.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited laptop processing power; shared Wi-Fi; high noise dorm.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but high mental clutter.
baseline.non_negotiables: No checking personal social media until the daily admin is clear; must archive or delete handled emails.

tasks.description: Search for keywords like 'Deadline', 'Exam', or 'Assignment'. Focus only on those for the first 5 minutes to ensure high-priority items are handled.
Respond to any email that takes less than 60 seconds to answer. If it takes longer, add it to a 'Later' folder and set a calendar reminder.
Find and unsubscribe from two promotional mailing lists that are cluttering the professional inbox. This provides long-term noise reduction.
Note the total number of unread emails. Seeing the number decrease provides the visual proof that the task is manageable.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying 'Spaced Repetition' to flashcards for midterms
description: Ben is a 19-year-old pre-law student who is drowning in Latin terminology and case law. He has a habit of 'cramming'—studying for 6 hours straight the night before a quiz—which leads to high stress and zero long-term retention. His friction point is 'Mental Friction'; he finds the initial struggle of remembering a card frustrating and often 'cheats' by looking at the answer too early. He also feels that 15 minutes is 'too short' to be productive, leading him to skip the habit entirely.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 30 minutes by bus; limited desk space in a quad-dorm; constant noise from roommates.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but prone to digital eye strain from excessive laptop use.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a dedicated Spaced Repetition System (SRS) app; cards must be reviewed before 11 AM to ensure the brain is fresh.

tasks.description: While waiting for the campus shuttle, open the app and download the day's 'due' cards. This ensures the material is ready the moment he sits down.
When a card appears, he must close his eyes and try to recall the answer for exactly 5 seconds before flipping. This 'desirable difficulty' is what builds the memory.
Any card that takes more than two attempts to remember must be 'flagged.' He will spend the last 2 minutes of the session specifically re-reading the textbook context for just those flagged cards.
Take a screenshot of the 'No more cards due' screen and send it to a study buddy. This external accountability makes the 15-minute habit feel like a finished 'mission'.

This 'morning static' makes her feel paralyzed, often leading her to stay in bed for an hour. Her friction point is 'Self-Censorship'—she tries to write 'pretty' or 'meaningful' journal entries, which makes the process feel like another graded assignment.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Morning yoga class at 7 AM; shares a room with a light sleeper; limited privacy.
baseline.physical_constraints: Elevated morning heart rate (anxiety-linked); shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a physical notebook; handwriting can be illegible; no one else is allowed to read it.

tasks.description: Set a timer for 60 seconds and write down every single 'to-do' or 'worry' currently in the head. No sentences required—just a list of fragments to clear the mental buffer.
Write one brutally honest sentence about how she feels right now (e.g., 'I am terrified of failing'). Naming the emotion reduces its power over her day.
Identify three actionable items from the 'Messy Minute' and move them to a sticky note. The rest of the list is mentally 'discarded' for the morning.
Physically close the notebook and place it in a drawer. This physical action signals to the brain that the 'worrying phase' is over and the 'action phase' has begun.

description: Kevin is a 22-year-old engineering senior who is brilliant on paper but 'collapses' in person. He mumbles, hunches his shoulders, and fidgets with his pen, making him appear nervous and unreliable to recruiters. He wants to project 'Executive Presence' but feels like a 'fraud' when he tries to sit up straight. His friction point is 'Physical Discomfort'—his back muscles are weak from years of gaming, making good posture literally tiring.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Interviews are usually on Zoom; limited professional attire; shared bedroom with no full-length mirror.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (forward head posture); tight hamstrings; weak core.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the laptop camera for self-review; sessions must be standing or seated-active (no lying down).

tasks.description: Stand against a wall with heels, glutes, and head touching. Perform 10 'Wall Angels' (sliding arms up and down) to open the chest and realign the spine.
Open the webcam. Practice sitting in the 'Power Box' (hands visible but not frantic, head in the upper third of the frame). Speak one paragraph of his 'About Me' intro.
While speaking on camera, keep hands resting flat on the desk or loosely clasped. If he feels the urge to fidget, he must squeeze his toes instead (invisible to the camera).
Practice looking directly into the camera lens (not the screen) and giving a brief, 2-second smile. This builds 'digital warmth' and trust with the interviewer.

goal: Engage in 10 minutes of 'Low-Impact' morning movement to manage back spasms
description: Zoe is a 20-year-old student who recently started having lower back 'spasms' from sitting 12 hours a day at a drafting table. She is scared of 'real' exercise because she doesn't want to make the pain worse. She feels 'clunky' and uncoordinated. Her friction point is 'Fear-Avoidance'—whenever her back feels slightly stiff, she stops moving entirely, which actually increases the stiffness.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor dorm with no elevator; limited floor space; uses a heavy backpack.
baseline.physical_constraints: Anterior pelvic tilt; limited hip mobility; tight hip flexors.
baseline.non_negotiables: No jumping; no heavy lifting; must use a yoga mat or a soft rug.

tasks.description: Perform 10 slow Cat-Cow cycles on the floor. Focus on the breath and moving one vertebra at a time to reduce the 'spasm' tension.
Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining a flat back (5 reps per side). This builds the 'internal corset' that supports her spine.
Perform a gentle lunge stretch for 30 seconds per side. This 'unlocks' the hips that have been shortened by a day of sitting.
Write down if the movement felt like 'Good Discomfort' (stretching) or 'Bad Pain' (sharp). Recognizing the difference helps her overcome her 'fear-avoidance'.

goal: Spend 10 minutes every morning performing 'Posture Reset' and lower-back stabilization
description: Zack is a 19-year-old student who spends 8 hours a day in non-ergonomic library chairs. He wakes up with a 'stiff' lower back and 'hunched' shoulders. He often 'snaps' at classmates because he’s in low-level pain all morning. His friction point is 'Gym Intimidation'—he thinks the only way to get fit is the campus gym, which he finds too loud and public, so he stays in pain.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor walk-up; limited floor space; uses a heavy 20lb backpack; high noise dorm.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (forward head posture); tight scalene muscles; shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be standing (no floor space); must be done before putting on the backpack; no equipment needed.

tasks.description: Stand against the back of the door and perform 15 chin tucks (creating a 'double chin'). This realigns the cervical spine after a night of poor pillow support.
Form a 'W' with the arms and squeeze the shoulder blades together for 10 seconds. Repeat 5 times. This opens the chest that has been 'collapsed' by drafting or typing.
Perform 4 rounds of Box Breathing (Inhale 4, Hold 4, Exhale 4, Hold 4) while standing. This physically lowers his stress baseline before he enters the quad.
Tighten both backpack straps so the bag sits high on the back. This uses the weight of his books to pull his shoulders back rather than down.

goal: Execute a 10-minute 'Morning Sunlight and Movement' routine for circadian health
description: Noah is a 21-year-old student living in a basement-level apartment. He has 'Delayed Sleep Phase Syndrome,' meaning he naturally wants to sleep until noon, causing him to miss 9 AM classes. His friction point is 'The Cave Reflex'—it is so dark and cold in his room that he feels a biological urge to stay under the covers, scrolling his phone for warmth and artificial light simulation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: No direct sunlight in the apartment; must walk 10 minutes to reach the campus quad; heavy winter weather.
baseline.physical_constraints: Extreme morning grogginess (sleep inertia); seasonal affective disorder (SAD).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be physically outside within 30 minutes of waking; no sunglasses allowed during the 10-minute light exposure.

tasks.description: Immediately upon the alarm, turn on every light in the room. This 'fakes' the sunrise to trigger the cortisol awakening response.
Place a warm coat and boots directly next to the bed the night before. This minimizes the 'friction' of getting dressed in a cold room.

Walk to the campus quad and spend 10 minutes looking at the sky (not directly at the sun). The lux levels from even a cloudy sky are far higher than any indoor light, signaling the brain to reset its internal clock.
At 11:00 PM, note how easy it was to fall asleep. He will notice that the morning light exposure makes him tired at a 'normal' time for the first time in years.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily applying 'Spaced Repetition' to active recall flashcards
description: Ben is a 19-year-old student drowning in technical terminology and case studies. He has a habit of 'cramming'—studying for 6 hours straight the night before a quiz—which leads to high stress and zero long-term retention. His friction point is 'Mental Friction'; he finds the initial struggle of remembering a card frustrating and often 'cheats' by looking at the answer too early.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 30 minutes by bus; limited desk space in a quad-dorm; constant noise from roommates.
baseline.physical_constraints: None, but prone to digital eye strain from excessive laptop use.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a dedicated SRS (Spaced Repetition System) app; cards must be reviewed before 11 AM.

tasks.description: While waiting for the campus shuttle, open the app and download the day's 'due' cards. This ensures the material is ready the moment he sits down.
When a card appears, he must close his eyes and try to recall the answer for exactly 5 seconds before flipping. This 'desirable difficulty' is what builds the memory.
Any card that takes more than two attempts to remember must be 'flagged.' He will spend the last 2 minutes of the session specifically re-reading the textbook context for just those flagged cards.
Take a screenshot of the 'No more cards due' screen. This external proof of completion makes the 15-minute habit feel like a finished 'mission'.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Professional Body Language' for senior job interviews
description: Kevin is a 22-year-old engineering senior who is brilliant on paper but 'collapses' in person. He mumbles, hunches his shoulders, and fidgets, making him appear nervous and unreliable to recruiters. His friction point is 'Physical Discomfort'—his back muscles are weak from years of gaming, making good posture literally tiring.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Interviews are usually on Zoom; limited professional attire; shared bedroom with no full-length mirror.
baseline.physical_constraints: Forward head posture; tight hamstrings; weak core.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the laptop camera for self-review; sessions must be standing or seated-active (no lying down).

tasks.description: Stand against a wall with heels, glutes, and head touching. Perform 10 'Wall Angels' (sliding arms up and down) to open the chest and realign the spine.
Open the webcam. Practice sitting in the 'Power Box' (hands visible but not frantic, head in the upper third of the frame). Speak one paragraph of his 'About Me' intro.
While speaking on camera, keep hands resting flat on the desk or loosely clasped. If he feels the urge to fidget, he must squeeze his toes instead (invisible to the camera).
Practice looking directly into the camera lens (not the screen) and giving a brief, 2-second smile. This builds 'digital warmth' and trust with the interviewer.

description: Zoe is a 20-year-old student who recently started having lower back 'spasms' from sitting 12 hours a day at a drafting table. She is scared of 'real' exercise because she doesn't want to make the pain worse. Her friction point is 'Fear-Avoidance'—whenever her back feels slightly stiff, she stops moving entirely, which actually increases the stiffness.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor dorm with no elevator; limited floor space; uses a heavy backpack.
baseline.physical_constraints: Anterior pelvic tilt; limited hip mobility; tight hip flexors.
baseline.non_negotiables: No jumping; no heavy lifting; must use a yoga mat or a soft rug.

tasks.description: Perform 10 slow Cat-Cow cycles on the floor. Focus on the breath and moving one vertebra at a time to reduce the 'spasm' tension.
Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining a flat back. This builds the 'internal corset' that supports her spine during long drafting sessions.
Perform a gentle lunge stretch for 30 seconds per side. This 'unlocks' the hips that have been shortened by a day of sitting.
Write down if the movement felt like 'Good Discomfort' (stretching) or 'Bad Pain' (sharp). Recognizing the difference helps her overcome her 'fear-avoidance'.

description: Zack is a 19-year-old student who spends 8 hours a day in non-ergonomic chairs. He wakes up with a 'stiff' lower back and 'hunched' shoulders. His friction point is 'Gym Intimidation'—he thinks the only way to get fit is the campus gym, which he finds too loud and public, so he stays in pain.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a top-floor walk-up; limited floor space; uses a heavy 20lb backpack; high noise dorm.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tech-neck (forward head posture); tight scalene muscles; shallow breathing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Exercises must be standing (no floor space); must be done before putting on the backpack; no equipment needed.

tasks.description: Stand against the back of the door and perform 15 chin tucks (creating a 'double chin'). This realigns the cervical spine after a night of poor pillow support.
Form a 'W' with the arms and squeeze the shoulder blades together for 10 seconds. This opens the chest that has been 'collapsed' by drafting or typing.
Perform 4 rounds of Box Breathing (Inhale 4, Hold 4, Exhale 4, Hold 4) while standing. This physically lowers his stress baseline before he enters the quad.
Tighten both backpack straps so the bag sits high on the back. This uses the weight of his books to pull his shoulders back rather than down.

goal: Spend 20 minutes daily conducting a 'Literature Audit' for a senior thesis
description: Elena is a 21-year-old Sociology senior. Her friction point is 'Search Result Paralysis'—whenever she opens Google Scholar, she finds 5,000 results and feels so behind that she closes the tab and watches TV instead. She needs to move from 'searching' to 'systematic auditing' so the mountain feels like a series of hills.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Thesis deadline in 3 months; working 15 hours/week at the library; shared computer access.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronic eye strain from reading PDFs; headaches triggered by blue light.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the university's VPN for database access; every paper must be logged in a spreadsheet; no 'skim-reading'—must read the abstract fully.

tasks.description: Using a specific keyword string (e.g., 'urban gentrification + displacement'), download exactly three papers. Stop once you reach three to prevent the 'infinite tab' trap.
Read the abstract of each paper and enter the core finding, methodology, and citation into a 'Literature Matrix' spreadsheet. This creates a searchable database for her future self.
Look at the bibliography of the best paper from today. Highlight one title to search for tomorrow. This ensures a logical flow to her research.
Close the laptop and look out the window for 60 seconds. This manages her eye strain and signals the end of the research block.

goal: Perform 10 minutes of Progressive Muscle Relaxation (PMR) before bedtime
description: Marcus is a 20-year-old Pre-law student with severe insomnia. His friction point is 'Cognitive Rumination'—the second his head hits the pillow, his brain starts replaying LSAT logic games. He has a physical habit of clenching his jaw and tensing his shoulders without realizing it, keeping his nervous system in a 'high-alert' state.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Noisy frat house environment; irregular sleep schedule due to study groups; limited budget for sleep aids.
baseline.physical_constraints: Bruxism (teeth grinding); recurring tension in the upper trapezius.
baseline.non_negotiables: No phone usage 30 minutes before the PMR; must be performed in a lying position; focus on 'breath-syncing' with muscle release.

tasks.description: Inhale and squeeze the toes and calves for 5 seconds, then exhale and release instantly. Notice the sensation of the blood returning. This 'grounds' the body away from the head.
Pull the shoulders up to the ears, hold with maximum tension, then drop them with a heavy exhale. This targets Marcus's specific area of chronic tension.
Clench the jaw and furrow the brow, then release and let the mouth hang slightly open. This addresses the bruxism directly.
Breathe naturally and scan the body for any remaining 'hot spots' of tension. If found, direct the exhale toward that area. This builds his interoceptive awareness.

goal: Spend 15 minutes daily practicing 'Whiteboard Problem Solving' for technical interviews
description: Maya is a 19-year-old CS student. Her friction point is 'Compiler Reliance'—she can't solve a problem without a debugger telling her what's wrong. She freezes during interviews when asked to explain her logic on a whiteboard. She often gets stuck, feels a rush of shame, and immediately looks up the solution on LeetCode.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited floor space; shares a room; no actual whiteboard (uses a tablet or paper).
baseline.physical_constraints: Hand cramping from grip tension when nervous.
baseline.non_negotiables: No access to an IDE or compiler during this block; must verbalize the Big O notation for every solution; must write the logic in pseudocode first.

tasks.description: After reading the prompt, write the logic in plain English/pseudocode. Do not think about syntax yet. This forces the brain to solve the logic, not the language.
Draw a table and manually track variable values for a small input (e.g., an array of 3 elements). This simulates the 'Human Debugger' mode required in interviews.
Identify three things that could break the code (e.g., null input, empty string, huge integers). Write these cases down. This shows the 'seniority' of her thinking.
Only now, look at the optimal solution. Compare the $O(n)$ complexity with her own. Log one specific trick she missed for tomorrow's review.

goal: Spend 10 minutes daily practicing 'Active Listening' in club meetings or seminars
description: Daniel is a 20-year-old Political Science student who wants to be a diplomat. His friction point is 'Interruption Reflex'—he is so eager to prove he is smart that he cuts people off mid-sentence to offer his own analysis. This makes his peers avoid him in group projects. He needs to learn that 'Listening is a high-status behavior.'

baseline.fixed_constraints: Has 3 seminars per week; highly competitive academic environment; high extroversion.
baseline.physical_constraints: Fidgets with his phone when he isn't the one talking.
baseline.non_negotiables: No speaking during the first 5 minutes of a group discussion; phone must be face-down on the table; must use the '2-second pause' rule.

tasks.description: When someone finishes speaking, wait for a full count of 'one-one-thousand, two-one-thousand' before opening his mouth. This ensures the other person is actually finished.
While someone else is talking, mentally summarize their point in 5 words. This forces the brain to process *their* data instead of generating *his* response.
Instead of stating an opinion, his first contribution must be a question about what the previous person said (e.g., 'What did you mean by X?'). This builds social capital.
After the meeting, record if the other person's body language seemed more or less open compared to previous weeks. Seeing the 'openness' score rise will motivate him.

goal: Execute a 10-minute 'Digital Art' recovery and eye-health routine
description: Sofia is a 19-year-old Digital Arts student. Her friction point is 'Flow-State Blindness'—she gets so absorbed in a drawing that she won't blink or move for 3 hours. She's starting to experience 'visual snow,' blurred vision, and a dull ache in her drawing wrist. She is terrified of 'Career-Ending Carpal Tunnel' but finds breaks 'boring' and disruptive to her creativity.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Drafting tablet is at a fixed height; shared studio space; deadlines for portofolio pieces.
baseline.physical_constraints: Beginning stages of wrist tendinitis; dry-eye syndrome.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the '20-20-20' rule; no drawing during the 10-minute recovery block; must use a physical timer (not an app on the drawing screen).

tasks.description: Look at an object 20 feet away for 20 seconds, and blink 20 times rapidly. This re-lubricates the eyes and prevents the 'staring' strain of detailed art.
Perform 10 slow nerve glides (making an 'OK' sign and flipping it to the eye). This prevents the drawing hand from going numb during long sessions.
Shake both hands vigorously for 30 seconds, then reach the arms behind the back to open the chest. This addresses the 'hunch' Sofia adopts while drawing.
Stand up and walk away from the screen to look at her work from 10 feet away. This provides a fresh creative perspective while forcing her to stay vertical.

goal: Establish a 15-minute daily 'Micro-Tidy' routine to prevent weekend chore paralysis
description: Leo is a 19-year-old freshman who suffers from 'Mount Washmore' syndrome. He waits until he has zero clean underwear and his desk is buried in snack wrappers before he cleans. This turns a simple chore into a 4-hour weekend ordeal that he dreads. His friction point is 'All-or-Nothing Thinking'—he feels that if he can't deep-clean the whole room, there's no point in throwing away a single wrapper.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Small shared dorm room; limited storage bins; trash room is down a long hallway.
baseline.physical_constraints: Dust allergies triggered by neglected spaces.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a 15-minute physical timer; when the timer rings, he must stop cleaning immediately even if the room isn't perfect.

tasks.description: Pick up all clothing items off the floor and chair. Put dirty items in the hamper and re-wearable items on a specific hook. This instantly reclaims floor space and reduces visual clutter.
Scan the desk and nightstand solely for actual garbage (wrappers, cans, old notes). Put them in a small bag. Ignore the textbooks or electronics for now.
Tie the trash bag and walk it down to the communal chute immediately. This physical exit from the room provides a concrete sense of completion.
When the timer goes off, stop immediately. Look at the reclaimed desk space. Acknowledge that a '15-minute clean' is better than a '0-minute clean.'

goal: Implement a '1-to-1' Water-to-Coffee ratio to eliminate afternoon dehydration headaches
description: Priya is a 20-year-old Nursing student stuck in the 'Caffeine Loop.' She relies on iced lattes to get through clinicals, gets dehydrated, develops a headache, and drinks another latte thinking she just needs more energy. Her friction point is 'Taste Aversion'—she associates plain water with 'being sick' or 'boring diets' and relies on sweet drinks for comfort during stressful shifts.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Long 12-hour shifts with limited bathroom breaks; wears heavy scrubs; campus cafes are highly accessible.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronic tension headaches; jittery hands from over-caffeination.
baseline.non_negotiables: No restricting coffee (she can drink it, but must earn it); must use a measured 16oz water bottle.

tasks.description: Before she is allowed to step into the campus cafe or press the button on the espresso machine, she must drink 8oz of water. The coffee becomes the 'reward' for the hydration.
For every sip of coffee taken during a lecture, she must take one sip of water from her bottle. This mechanically slows down her caffeine intake and prevents the sudden spike/crash.
Set a daily alarm for 3:00 PM. After this time, all beverages must be caffeine-free (herbal tea, sparkling water). This protects her sleep architecture.
Monitor urine color during bathroom breaks. A pale yellow color acts as visual, biological proof that the 1-to-1 ratio is successfully preventing the dehydration headache.

goal: Maintain a 5-minute daily 'Competence Log' to combat academic Imposter Syndrome
description: Malik is a 21-year-old Computer Science junior. He spends his breaks looking at his peers' GitHub profiles and LinkedIn updates. He feels like a 'fraud' who accidentally got into the program and will be exposed soon. His friction point is 'The Comparison Trap'—he compares his unpolished, behind-the-scenes struggles to the curated, final highlight reels of his classmates, paralyzing his ability to code.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Highly competitive major with graded curves; heavily involved in group coding projects.
baseline.physical_constraints: Stress-induced insomnia.
baseline.non_negotiables: Log must be private; entries must be strictly factual (no self-deprecating caveats allowed).

tasks.description: Open a dedicated document and write down one objective technical win from the day (e.g., 'I successfully debugged the API connection error after 3 tries'). Facts only.
Write one sentence comparing today's knowledge to his knowledge 6 months ago (e.g., 'Six months ago, I didn't know what a JSON payload was'). This shifts comparison from peers to his past self.
Use a browser extension to block LinkedIn and peer GitHub repositories during his 2-hour study block. He cannot trigger his Imposter Syndrome if he removes the source material.
Read the log entry out loud. Conclude by stating: 'I am a student. My job is to learn, not to know everything already.' This resets the baseline expectation.

goal: Execute a 10-minute 'Delegation & Status' check to manage group project burnout
description: Emma is a 22-year-old Business student and a chronic 'Control Freak.' In group projects, she doesn't trust her peers to do the work correctly, so she stays up until 3 AM re-doing their slides. She is exhausted and resentful. Her friction point is 'Perfectionism Overlap'—she equates a B+ project with personal failure, making her unable to tolerate the different working styles of her teammates.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Three simultaneous group projects this semester; strict grading rubrics; team members with varying schedules.
baseline.physical_constraints: Burnout-related fatigue; tension in the jaw.
baseline.non_negotiables: She is not allowed to edit another person's slide until 24 hours before the deadline; communication must be in writing.

tasks.description: Send a polite, clear message in the group chat asking one specific person for one specific item with a deadline (e.g., 'Hey John, can you drop the market analysis stats in the doc by Tuesday?').
Review the shared document. If a teammate's work is 'good enough' (even if not perfect), leave a positive comment and *close the tab*. Do not rewrite it.
Ask herself: 'If we get an 85% instead of a 95% on this presentation, will it ruin my career?' The answer is no. This cognitive reframe lowers the high stakes she places on group work.
Mute the group chat notifications at 8:00 PM. No more project management for the evening. Her teammates' late-night working habits do not have to become her emergency.

goal: Read 10 pages of 'Non-Academic Fiction' daily to recover from reading burnout
description: Tariq is a 20-year-old Literature major who used to love reading but now associates all books with essays, grades, and intellectual critique. He hasn't read for fun in two years. His friction point is 'Academic Overspill'—when he tries to read a novel, his brain automatically starts looking for themes and motifs to analyze, turning a relaxing hobby back into 'work.'

baseline.fixed_constraints: Required to read 200+ pages of heavy academic text per week; tired eyes by the end of the day.
baseline.physical_constraints: Mental fatigue; digital eye strain.
baseline.non_negotiables: The book must be a genre he does *not* study in class (e.g., sci-fi, thriller, or fantasy); no highlighters or pens allowed near the book.

tasks.description: At 10:00 PM, put the phone on the charger across the room and pick up the physical fiction book. This replaces the endless scrolling loop with a finite, tactile activity.
Read exactly 10 pages. If the brain starts trying to 'analyze' the author's prose, actively tell it: 'We are just here to find out what happens next.' Treat it like watching a movie.
If he isn't enjoying the book after 3 days, he must put it down (Do Not Finish) and pick a new one. He is not being graded; there is no penalty for quitting a boring book.
Close the book and note how the 'world' of the novel provided a 20-minute vacation from his campus stresses. This reinforces the value of reading for pleasure.

goal: Improve sleep hygiene and morning alertness
description: The user is a software engineer working remotely who has fallen into a 'revenge bedtime procrastination' cycle, staying up until 2 AM scrolling on their phone because they feel they lack 'me time' during the day. They struggle with brain fog until noon and feel guilty about their lack of discipline.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Daily stand-up meeting at 9:30 AM; living in a studio apartment where the bed is visible from the desk.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronic lower back pain that flares up if sitting too long; high caffeine sensitivity after 3 PM.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be online by 9:00 AM for core work hours; must care for a senior dog who needs a short walk before 8:00 AM.

tasks.description: Place the smartphone in a charging station located in the kitchen area at least 45 minutes before intended sleep. This addresses the emotional urge to scroll by physically separating the device.
Engage in a non-digital activity such as reading a physical book, sketching, or folding laundry. This provides the 'me time' sensation without the blue light or dopamine spikes of social media.
Immediately after the dog walk, spend time by the window or step onto the balcony. This helps reset the circadian rhythm despite the initial morning grogginess.
Review the previous week's sleep consistency and phone usage. If a 'relapse' occurred, identify the emotional trigger (e.g., a stressful meeting) rather than resorting to self-criticism.
Perform a sequence of 3 gentle stretches to manage back pain, reducing physical discomfort that often makes falling asleep difficult later that night.

goal: Downsize and relocate to a smaller apartment in a new city
description: The user is moving for a new job opportunity. They are feeling overwhelmed by the sentimental value of their belongings and the logistical nightmare of a long-distance move while still working 40+ hours a week. They struggle with 'decision fatigue' when sorting through items.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lease ends on the 30th; new job starts on the 1st of the following month.
baseline.physical_constraints: Limited lifting capacity due to a previous shoulder injury; no elevator in the current building.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must keep professional wardrobe and essential kitchen equipment; must stay within a strict $2,000 moving budget.

tasks.description: Spend 2 hours each weekend sorting one specific area. Focus on objective utility to combat emotional attachment to items not used in over a year.

tasks.task: Coordinate professional movers or truck rental
tasks.description: Obtain three quotes and book the service. This must be done early to avoid last-minute price hikes or lack of availability.
Systematically update the address for banks, insurance, and government IDs, and schedule the shut-off/turn-on dates for electricity and internet.
Create a clearly labeled box containing toiletries, 2 days of clothes, chargers, and basic tools to reduce stress upon arrival at the new apartment.
Perform a final check of all cabinets and a basic cleaning of the empty unit to ensure the security deposit is returned.

goal: Establish a consistent 'Financial Friday' budget review
description: A freelancer with fluctuating monthly income feels constant anxiety about their bank balance. They tend to avoid looking at their accounts when they know they've overspent, leading to a cycle of shame and late fees.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Income arrives at irregular intervals; several recurring subscriptions are tied to different credit cards.
baseline.physical_constraints: No office space; works from a kitchen table which is often cluttered.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must set aside 25% of all gross income for quarterly taxes.

tasks.description: Log into all accounts and categorize the past week's spending. This confronts the 'avoidance' urge by making the data visible and neutral.
Calculate 25% of any payments received during the week and move that amount to a dedicated high-yield savings account.
Check for any 'zombie' subscriptions or upcoming renewals. Cancel one service that hasn't been used in the last 30 days.
Based on the week's earnings, adjust the 'fun money' or grocery budget for the following week to ensure no debt is accrued.

goal: Plan and execute a 10-year high school reunion
description: The user volunteered to lead the reunion committee but is struggling with low engagement from other alumni and the pressure of social comparison. They feel socially anxious about seeing old peers while also being the person 'in charge'.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Venue must be booked 3 months in advance; limited to local options to keep ticket prices low.
baseline.physical_constraints: Works full-time, so planning can only happen on Tuesday/Thursday evenings.
baseline.non_negotiables: The event must be break-even; no personal funds can be risked on the deposit.

tasks.task: Alumni interest survey and contact list
tasks.description: Create a digital form to gauge interest in dates and price points, then distribute via social media groups.
Visit two local venues and secure a date. Attempt to negotiate a 'no-money-down' reservation or a low-cost minimum spend to mitigate financial risk.
Configure an online payment platform to collect funds directly, ensuring transparency and automated record-keeping.

tasks.task: Event program and 'Memory Lane' asset collection
tasks.description: Solicit old photos and organize a simple playlist/slideshow. This task often feels tedious, so it's broken into smaller chunks.
Send out the final itinerary to attendees and assign simple tasks (like nametag distribution) to two other classmates.

goal: Integrate daily mindfulness into a high-stress management role
description: A middle manager feels constantly 'on edge' and reactive. They find traditional 20-minute meditation impossible because their mind wanders to their inbox immediately. They need a way to build resilience without feeling like they are 'wasting time'.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Back-to-back meetings from 10 AM to 4 PM; no private office (open-plan floor).
baseline.physical_constraints: Often skips lunch due to workload; experiences tension headaches by late afternoon.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be reachable via Slack for urgent escalations during work hours.

tasks.description: Take three deep, intentional breaths before clicking 'Join' on any virtual meeting or entering a conference room. This creates a micro-break in reactivity.
Eat lunch away from the desk for at least 15 minutes without looking at a phone. Focus entirely on the taste and texture of the food to ground the nervous system.
Spend 5 minutes writing down all unfinished tasks and stressors before leaving the office to prevent 'work-thought' bleed-over into evening rest.
Listen to a short guided meditation or educational podcast about stress physiology while commuting. This helps reframe stress as a manageable biological response.

goal: Rebuild a damaged friendship after a long period of silence
description: After a misunderstanding and two years of no contact, the user wants to reconnect with a former best friend. They feel a mix of guilt, fear of rejection, and uncertainty about how much the other person has changed.

baseline.fixed_constraints: The friend lives in a different time zone; no mutual friends remain to act as intermediaries.
baseline.physical_constraints: User has a tendency to over-analyze text messages, leading to 'analysis paralysis'.
baseline.non_negotiables: The user will stop reaching out if the friend explicitly asks for space or remains non-responsive after three attempts.

tasks.description: Send a short, no-obligation message (text or email) mentioning a shared memory or a simple update. Avoid asking for an immediate meeting.
If the friend responds, focus entirely on their life and updates without immediately pivoting back to the user's life or the past conflict.
Once a rapport is re-established, initiate a vulnerable conversation to apologize for the user's part in the previous falling out, without demanding an apology in return.
Interact with the friend's life in small ways (e.g., commenting on a post, sending a relevant article) to show consistent interest rather than sporadic intensity.

goal: Prepare and submit a career-pivot portfolio for UX Design
description: A former middle-school teacher is trying to transition into tech. They struggle with 'imposter syndrome' and feel their past experience is irrelevant. They have two unfinished case studies and feel paralyzed by the high visual standards of industry portfolios.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must finish before the spring hiring cycle begins in 6 weeks; only 10 hours of child-free time per week.
baseline.physical_constraints: Limited desk space; sharing a laptop with a spouse who also works from home.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must include at least 3 distinct projects; cannot afford a $2,000+ bootcamp.

tasks.description: Map teaching responsibilities (lesson planning, student empathy, stakeholder meetings) to UX competencies. This combats the 'zero experience' narrative.
Write the narrative for one project in a plain text document, focusing on the problem-solving process rather than the final visuals.
Select a website builder and apply a standard, clean template. This prevents 'design-looping' where the user spends weeks tweaking fonts instead of content.
Send a polite request to two working designers on a networking platform for a 15-minute 'brutal' portfolio audit.
Ensure all buttons work, proofread for 'teacher-speak' jargon, and publish the live link to LinkedIn.

goal: Maintain a low-impact exercise routine for chronic pain management
description: The user has mild fibromyalgia and often wakes up with significant joint stiffness. They tend to over-exercise on 'good days' which triggers a multi-day flare-up, leading to a cycle of 'boom and bust' that kills motivation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Morning stiffness lasts roughly 90 minutes; cannot do high-impact movements (jumping/running).
baseline.physical_constraints: Variable energy levels; weather changes significantly impact pain intensity.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must stop any activity immediately if nerve pain reaches a 'level 4' out of 10.

tasks.description: Perform 5 minutes of gentle joint rotations and stretching before even getting out of bed to signal the body it's safe to move.
A slow-paced walk around the block. If a flare-up is occurring, this is swapped for 10 minutes of restorative floor yoga.
Compare daily activity logs with pain levels. Identify if 'good day' over-exertion caused 'bad day' crashes to adjust the next week's intensity.
Prepare simple, easy-to-grab snacks (like pre-cut veggies or nuts) to ensure nutrition doesn't slip during high-pain, low-energy windows.

goal: Organize a chaotic family digital photo archive
description: A parent has 15,000+ unsorted photos spread across three old phones, two cloud services, and a physical hard drive. They fear losing memories of their children's early years but are paralyzed by the sheer volume of duplicates and blurry shots.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited storage on the primary cloud account; slow home upload speeds.
baseline.physical_constraints: Subject to 'emotional distraction' (stopping to look at every photo for 5 minutes).
baseline.non_negotiables: Must maintain a secondary physical backup (HDD) in addition to the cloud.

tasks.description: Choose one specific year (starting with the oldest) and move all photos from all devices into a single 'Year' folder. Do not delete anything yet.
Use a duplicate-finder tool, then manually delete blurry shots or accidental screenshots. Focus on keeping the 'story' rather than every frame.
Rename the top 10% of 'favorite' photos from the batch with descriptive names (e.g., '2015_Birthday_Leo.jpg') for easier searching later.
Trigger the final upload to the primary cloud and the external hard drive for the newly cleaned batch.

goal: Host a sustainable 15-person neighborhood dinner party
description: The user wants to foster community in their apartment complex without contributing to waste. They are anxious about social dynamics between different neighbors and 'eco-perfectionism' regarding the menu.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Small galley kitchen with only two burners; limited seating (requires neighbors to bring chairs).
baseline.physical_constraints: User has high social anxiety and tends to over-work, leaving them too tired to enjoy the actual party.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be zero-waste (no disposable plates/cutlery); must accommodate one vegan and one nut-allergy guest.

tasks.description: Send a digital invite asking guests to RSVP with dietary needs and—crucially—asking to borrow three folding chairs and extra silverware.
Design a 'one-pot' style meal (like a large lentil stew) that can be made in bulk using bulk-bin ingredients to minimize packaging.

tasks.task: Bulk Grocery and Farmers Market Run
tasks.description: Purchase all ingredients using reusable bags/jars. This requires a specific trip to a store that allows bulk containers.
Chop all vegetables and prepare the base sauce the night before. This ensures the host isn't cooking while guests arrive.
Enlist two neighbors to help with the non-disposable cleanup immediately after the meal to prevent the host from feeling resentful of the mess.

goal: Build a consistent 'Low-Tech' morning routine for a writer
description: A freelance writer finds themselves checking emails and news headlines the moment they wake up, which 'scrambles' their creative focus for the rest of the day. They want to reclaim their first hour for deep thought.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Partner wakes up at the same time and usually turns on the TV; must start paid client work by 8:30 AM.
baseline.physical_constraints: Coffee addiction requires a 10-minute brewing process; prone to 'morning gloom' without sunlight.
baseline.non_negotiables: No digital screens (phone, laptop, TV) for the first 60 minutes of the day.

tasks.description: Focus entirely on the sensory experience of making coffee—the smell of the beans, the sound of the water—to practice mindfulness before writing.
Write three pages of stream-of-consciousness thought in a physical notebook. This clears out the 'mental cobwebs' without the pressure of a digital cursor.
Do 5 minutes of jumping jacks or a short walk without headphones. The goal is to let the brain 'idle' and generate ideas naturally.
Only after the hour is up, open the laptop and spend 10 minutes specifically looking for urgent client emergencies before starting creative work.

goal: Transition from passive social media scrolling to active book reading
description: A former avid reader has lost their 'reading muscle' due to a high-stress corporate job and a habit of doom-scrolling before bed. They feel intellectually stagnant and guilty about the stack of unread books on their nightstand. They struggle with a short attention span and feel 'too tired' for complex plots in the evening.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Commutes 45 minutes by train each way; has a 1-hour lunch break that is often interrupted by Slack pings.
baseline.physical_constraints: Eye strain from 8+ hours of screen time daily; bedroom lighting is currently too dim for comfortable reading.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must read physical or e-ink books only (no reading on the smartphone) to reduce blue light exposure.

tasks.description: Open a book immediately upon sitting on the train before pulling out the phone. The goal is to finish at least 5 pages before the first stop, capitalizing on the transition period.
Every morning, place the current book on top of the pillow. This physical obstacle serves as a visual cue and reminder to read instead of scrolling once in bed.
Read 10 minutes of a 'fun' genre (thriller, graphic novel, or essay collection) that doesn't require high cognitive load. This helps rebuild the habit without the intimidation of 'heavy' literature.
Evaluate if the current book is enjoyable. If it feels like a chore, give permission to drop it and start another. This prevents 'reading slumps' caused by guilt over boring books.

goal: Deepen comprehension through a slow-reading and note-taking habit
description: A graduate student feels they 'read but don't retain.' They rush through non-fiction books to hit a target count, but can't recall key concepts a week later. They want to shift from quantity to quality, integrating a reflective practice into their daily routine.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Needs to manage a heavy academic load alongside personal reading; limited quiet space in a shared house.
baseline.physical_constraints: Hand cramps when writing for long periods; prefers typing but finds it distracting.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must produce at least one summary or 'evergreen note' per book finished.

tasks.description: While reading, underline key sentences and write at least two brief reactions or questions in the margins per chapter. This transforms the user from a passive consumer to an active participant.
Close the book and spend 5 minutes explaining the core argument of the day's reading out loud to an imaginary person. This 'Feynman Technique' highlights gaps in understanding.
Transfer the most important marginalia and highlights into a digital knowledge base (like Obsidian or Notion). Re-phrase the ideas in the user's own words.
