Review the last 14 days of notes and look for connections between different books or current real-world projects. This builds a 'latticework' of mental models.

goal: Build a shared family reading culture with young children
description: A parent wants to reduce their children's screen time and foster a love for stories. However, the parent is often exhausted by 7 PM and the kids are 'wound up' and resistant to sitting still. The user needs a sustainable way to make reading the 'fun' part of the night rather than a mandatory task.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Bedtime routine must be completed by 8:00 PM to ensure kids get enough sleep for school.
baseline.physical_constraints: Shared bedroom for two children with different reading levels; parent often loses their voice if reading for too long.
baseline.non_negotiables: No screens allowed in the bedroom after dinner; must include both children in the activity.

tasks.description: Pick 5 books from the local library every weekend and place them in a visible, accessible basket in the living room. Let the children choose the 'Book of the Night' to increase their sense of autonomy.
Read one picture book or one chapter of a middle-grade novel. Incorporate different voices for characters to maintain engagement and combat the parent's own fatigue through play.
On weekend mornings, the parent sits on the sofa and reads their own book while the kids look at theirs nearby. This demonstrates that reading is a lifelong adult pleasure, not just a 'kid thing'.
Play a high-quality audiobook during the cleanup phase of dinner/bath time. This hooks the children into a story before the physical book is even opened.

goal: Utilize audiobooks to integrate literature into a physical labor routine
description: A landscaper who works 10-hour days outdoors wants to read more but is too physically exhausted to hold a book or stay awake while reading at night. They want to turn their 'dead time' at work into a mobile classroom/library.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Works in noisy environments with heavy machinery; must remain aware of surroundings for safety.
baseline.physical_constraints: Constant movement; hands are often dirty or occupied; requires sweat-proof equipment.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use 'open-ear' or one-earbud-only listening for safety; cannot look at a screen while working.

tasks.description: While the truck is warming up, select the audiobook and ensure the playback speed is comfortable (e.g., 1.2x). This prevents fumbling with a phone mid-task.
Listen to the book during repetitive, low-risk tasks (mowing, weeding). If a task requires high focus, the audio is paused immediately to ensure quality of work and safety.
During the 30-minute lunch break, record a quick voice memo or type a one-sentence summary of what was heard so far to anchor the information.
Browse the digital library app once a week to place holds on popular titles. This ensures a steady stream of content without having to pay for every book.

goal: Establish a diverse 'Global Reading' habit
description: The user realized all the books they read are by authors from their own country. They want to expand their worldview by reading authors from every continent over the next year, but find it difficult to discover translated works and stick with unfamiliar cultural contexts.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Limited access to international bookstores; relies on digital platforms or ordering online.
baseline.physical_constraints: English is their primary language, so they are limited to works translated into or written in English.
baseline.non_negotiables: At least 50% of the year's reading must be by authors from the Global South; must not spend more than $30/month on books.

tasks.description: Dedicate time each Sunday to research prize-winning authors from a specific, pre-selected country. Use resources like the 'International Booker Prize' or 'Words Without Borders'.

Commit to reading at least 50 pages of a new international book every evening. This allows the user to adjust to new naming conventions or non-Western narrative structures without giving up prematurely.

When a book references a historical event or cultural practice the user doesn't understand, spend 10 minutes reading a brief overview online. This prevents the 'feeling lost' sensation that leads to quitting.
Update a physical or digital map by coloring in the country of the author just finished. This provides a visual sense of accomplishment and highlights 'blind spots' in the reading habit.

goal: Rebuild financial stability after a period of unemployment
description: The user recently started a new job after six months of being out of work. They have significant credit card debt and have developed 'scarcity trauma,' alternating between extreme frugality and impulsive 'treat yourself' spending. They need to stabilize their cash flow without feeling deprived.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Rent and utilities consume 45% of take-home pay; high-interest debt requires a minimum payment of $300/month.
baseline.physical_constraints: Commutes via public transit; relies on a 4-year-old laptop that may need replacement soon.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must contribute 3% to 401k to get the employer match; cannot use credit cards for any new purchases.

tasks.description: Log into the banking app every morning to review the previous day's spending. This normalizes looking at numbers and reduces the 'fear of the balance' developed during unemployment.
For any purchase over $20 that isn't groceries or bills, wait exactly 24 hours before buying. This builds a buffer against impulsive emotional spending.
On payday, immediately transfer a pre-set amount (even if small) to the credit card with the highest interest rate. This creates a psychological win through consistent progress.
Schedule one free or low-cost social activity (e.g., a park walk with a friend, a library visit). This addresses the emotional need for 'treats' without damaging the budget.

goal: Cultivate a 'Kitchen-Close' routine for better home organization
description: A busy parent of toddlers finds the kitchen is always a disaster by evening. The sight of dirty dishes in the morning causes immediate stress and sets a chaotic tone for the day. They struggle with low energy after the kids go to bed and tend to 'give up' and leave the mess for tomorrow.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dishwasher is small and requires strategic loading; toddler bedtime is highly unpredictable.
baseline.physical_constraints: Lower back pain from standing all day; kitchen has very little counter space.
baseline.non_negotiables: The sink must be empty before the user goes to sleep to prevent pests and morning anxiety.

tasks.description: Immediately after eating, load the largest pots and pans into the dishwasher or soak them. This reduces the visual 'mountain' that causes late-night overwhelm.
Set a timer for 10 minutes once the kids are asleep. Wipe counters, put away dry dishes, and clear the sink. The timer creates a 'finish line' for a tired brain.
While the coffee brews, unload the dishwasher. This ensures the sink stays clear during the breakfast rush because there is a place for dirty dishes to go immediately.
Toss expired fridge items and scrub the sink basin. This prevents the 'habit' from becoming just a superficial move-around of clutter.

goal: Build a sustainable 'Second Language' immersion habit
description: A professional wants to learn Spanish to better communicate with colleagues. They have tried apps before but find the 'gamified' streaks stressful and the learning doesn't stick. They need to integrate the language into their actual life rather than treating it like a chore.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Work days are long; limited energy for intensive grammar drills; no native speakers in their immediate household.
baseline.physical_constraints: Often has 'screen fatigue' by the end of the day.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must focus on 'Business Spanish' as well as casual conversation; budget limited to one streaming subscription or app.

tasks.description: Listen to a Spanish-language podcast or radio station during the morning commute or while doing chores. Focus on the 'music' of the language rather than perfect comprehension.
Throughout the day, try to name 5 objects you see or 3 actions you are doing in the target language. If you don't know the word, look it up immediately and say it out loud.
Watch 20 minutes of a show in Spanish with Spanish subtitles (no English). This connects visual cues with auditory patterns in a relaxed setting.
Record a 2-minute voice memo of yourself talking about your week in Spanish. Listen back to identify pronunciation hurdles and track progress over months.

goal: Maintain a 'Socially Active' lifestyle as an introverted remote worker
description: The user works from home and can go days without a face-to-face conversation. While they value their solitude, they've noticed increasing feelings of isolation and 'social rustiness.' They want to build a habit of connection that doesn't feel performative or draining.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a suburban area requiring a car for most outings; social battery is drained quickly by large crowds.
baseline.physical_constraints: Exhausted by the end of the work week; prone to 'canceling plans' at the last minute.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must include at least one in-person interaction per week that isn't a service transaction (like grocery shopping).

tasks.description: Work from a library or quiet coffee shop for 2 hours. Even without direct conversation, 'social snacking' (being around others) helps mitigate isolation.
Send a text, voice note, or meme to one friend you haven't spoken to in a while. This keeps the 'social pipes' open without the pressure of a full phone call.
Schedule and attend one recurring in-person meeting with a friend or neighbor (e.g., a Tuesday morning coffee). Recurring plans reduce the 'decision friction' of scheduling.
Reflect on which interactions felt restorative and which felt draining. Use this to say 'no' more effectively to big events and 'yes' to small ones.

goal: Integrate 'Micro-Nature' breaks for urban mental health
description: An urban dweller in a concrete-heavy neighborhood feels 'boxed in' and anxious. They want to connect with nature more regularly but feel that a 'hike' is too big an undertaking for a daily habit. They need to find 'nature' in the small gaps of a city life.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Nearest large park is a 20-minute drive away; high noise pollution in the apartment.
baseline.physical_constraints: Suffers from seasonal allergies; work schedule is strictly 9-5 with little flexibility.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must find a way to incorporate 'green' or 'blue' space visibility at least 5 times a week.

tasks.description: Spend 5 minutes looking at the sky or a potted plant while drinking morning tea. This 'soft fascination' helps clear the mind before the digital day begins.
When walking to the store or transit, take a route that has more trees or a small community garden, even if it adds 3 minutes to the trip.
Water, prune, or simply observe indoor houseplants. This hands-on interaction with living things provides a grounding physical sensation.
If traffic noise is high, play 15 minutes of high-quality forest or ocean sounds while decompressing after work. This 'auditory nature' helps lower cortisol levels.

goal: Design and launch a personalized 'legacy' recipe book for the family
description: The user’s grandmother is in the early stages of memory loss, and the user wants to preserve her undocumented 'pinch of this' recipes. The project feels heavy because of the emotional stakes and the chaotic nature of the grandmother's kitchen notes. The user is a perfectionist who fears they won't 'get it right' before it's too late.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be finished before the family reunion in 3 months; grandmother's energy peaks only between 10 AM and 1 PM.
baseline.physical_constraints: Recipes are written on stained, fading scraps of paper in a mix of two languages.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must include photos of the actual handwritten notes, not just typed versions; must produce at least 5 physical copies.

tasks.description: Scan or photograph all existing physical recipe scraps and sort them into digital folders by meal type. This ensures the originals are safe even if the project stalls.
Spend a morning cooking one 'legendary' dish with the grandmother. Measure her 'handfuls' and 'pinches' using standard cups and spoons to create a replicable version.
Select the top 20 recipes and write a short paragraph for each, detailing the family memory associated with it. This moves the book from a manual to a legacy piece.
Upload content to a self-publishing platform. Choose a template that prioritizes legibility over complex design to reduce 'perfectionist paralysis'.
Order a single proof copy to check for errors in measurements or photo quality before committing to the full family order.

goal: Successfully navigate the 'First 90 Days' of a promotion to Senior Manager
description: The user was promoted from a high-performing individual contributor to a manager of their former peers. They are struggling with the shift from 'doing' to 'leading' and feel the need to prove themselves by working 12-hour days. They are on the verge of burnout and fear losing the respect of their friends on the team.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must attend 15+ hours of leadership meetings per week; responsible for the quarterly budget delivery.
baseline.physical_constraints: Suffering from tension headaches due to increased screen time and stress.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must conduct weekly 1-on-1s with 8 direct reports; cannot work on Sundays.

tasks.description: Hard-block two 90-minute sessions per week for long-term strategy and budget planning. During this time, Slack and email notifications must be turned off.
Conduct individual meetings with each team member. Focus 80% of the time on their blockers and career goals rather than status updates.
Physically close the laptop and write down the 'Top 3' priorities for the next day. This signals to the brain that the work day is done, reducing evening anxiety.
Meet with a senior mentor outside the immediate department to discuss leadership challenges and get an objective perspective on team dynamics.

goal: Prepare a home for a new foster dog with behavioral trauma
description: A long-time dog owner is taking in a 'fear-reactive' rescue. The user is anxious about their current cat’s safety and whether they have the emotional patience for a dog that might not want to be touched. They feel a mix of excitement and a 'what have I done?' dread.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dog arrives in 2 weeks; the apartment is currently open-plan with no built-in dividers.
baseline.physical_constraints: The dog is 60lbs and may pull on the leash; user has a small balcony but no private yard.
baseline.non_negotiables: The cat and dog must be kept physically separated at all times for the first 7 days.

tasks.description: Clear a quiet corner of the bedroom and set up a crate or high-walled bed. Cover it with a blanket to create a 'den' where the dog can retreat and feel unobserved.
Install extra-tall baby gates with 'cat doors' to allow the resident cat to move freely while keeping the dog contained in specific rooms.
Walk the neighborhood at 6 AM and 9 PM to identify the quietest paths with the fewest other dogs or loud noises to prepare for the first few 'scary' walks.
Purchase and prep specific tools: long-leash, front-clip harness, and 'stinky' high-value treats (like liver or cheese) for positive reinforcement training.
Conduct a final call with the foster coordinator to review the dog’s specific bite history or triggers to ensure no 'surprises' on day one.

goal: Reclaim health after a major illness through a 'Walk-to-Run' progression
description: After recovering from a severe respiratory infection, the user—a former half-marathoner—finds themselves getting winded just going up stairs. They are depressed by their lost fitness and tend to push too hard, causing 'relapses' in fatigue. They need to rebuild from zero with extreme patience.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must work a desk job 8 hours a day; winter weather makes outdoor exercise difficult for sensitive lungs.
baseline.physical_constraints: Significant loss of lung capacity; heart rate spikes quickly; post-viral fatigue syndrome concerns.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use a heart rate monitor and stay below 130 BPM; must stop if dizziness occurs.

tasks.description: A flat-ground walk at a slow pace. The goal isn't speed, but consistent movement for 15 minutes to test cardiovascular response.
Spend 10 minutes focused on deep, belly-breathing to expand lung capacity and calm the nervous system, which often stays in 'fight or flight' after illness.
Record not just the exercise, but the 'crash' levels the next day. This data helps the user realize that rest is a productive part of the training.
Every two weeks, if the 'energy log' is positive, introduce 30 seconds of very light jogging for every 5 minutes of walking.

goal: Declutter and digitize 20 years of paper records
description: The user has five large filing cabinets filled with old taxes, medical records, and instruction manuals. They are moving to a smaller home soon and are overwhelmed by the 'paper weight.' They find the task incredibly boring and tend to quit after 10 minutes.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must keep physical copies of the last 7 years of taxes; identity theft is a major concern (requires shredding).
baseline.physical_constraints: The cabinets are in a dusty basement, which triggers the user's allergies.
baseline.non_negotiables: All shredded paper must be properly recycled; digital files must be encrypted and backed up in two locations.

tasks.description: Select one drawer and sort into three piles: Shred, Recycle, or Scan. By limiting the scope, the user avoids the 'whole house' overwhelm.
Scan the 'Keep' pile using a high-speed feeder or mobile app. Use a consistent naming convention (YYYY_Category_Subject) to make the files searchable.
Spend 20 minutes feeding the shredder while listening to a favorite podcast or music. This 'habit stacking' makes the monotonous task bearable.
Ensure the week's scans have synced to the cloud and the offline encrypted drive. Delete the local temp files to keep the computer clean.

goal: Launch a community-supported tool-sharing library
description: The user wants to reduce local waste and help neighbors by creating a lending library for high-cost, low-frequency tools (drills, ladders, pressure washers). They are passionate but prone to 'vision creep,' wanting to do too much at once, and they fear the legal liability of someone getting hurt using a shared tool.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must find a host location (community center or garage) that is accessible; must follow local zoning laws for non-profits.
baseline.physical_constraints: Tools are heavy; requires a vehicle for transport during the initial collection phase.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must have a signed liability waiver for every member; must have a digital tracking system to prevent loss.

tasks.description: Create a simple digital survey to see what tools neighbors actually need and what they are willing to donate. This prevents storing junk no one uses.
Consult a pro-bono legal resource or use an existing 'Tool Library' template to draft a liability waiver and research small-business insurance.
Select an open-source library management software and catalog the first 10 donated items, including photos and condition notes.
Secure a 10x10 space in a local garage or center and install heavy-duty shelving. This creates a physical 'anchor' for the project.
Invite the first 20 survey respondents to join as 'Founding Members' to test the check-out process and refine the rules of use.

goal: Master the basics of modular synthesis and sound design
description: The user is a hobbyist musician who bought a complex synthesizer but feels intimidated by the 'spaghetti' of patch cables. They often turn it on, get frustrated by making 'unpleasant noise,' and go back to simple presets. They want to understand the physics of sound rather than just stumbling onto good luck.

baseline.fixed_constraints: The synthesizer is in a home studio; limited to 1 hour of 'loud' noise per day due to thin apartment walls.
baseline.physical_constraints: Small knobs require fine motor control; eye strain from focusing on small labels in low light.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use headphones for 80% of practice; must learn 'Subtractive' synthesis principles before moving to 'FM'.

tasks.description: Focus on only one part of the synth (e.g., the Filter) and explore its full range of sounds for 15 minutes. Resist the urge to plug in everything at once.
Take a photo of a successful 'patch' and write down why it works. This builds a personal library of techniques to replace 'happy accidents.'
Read one chapter of a synthesis manual or watch a technical video, then immediately try to recreate that specific waveform or modulation on the gear.
Spend 45 minutes just playing without the goal of recording or learning. This preserves the 'fun' of the hobby and prevents academic burnout.

goal: Develop a consistent 'Slow-Cook' meal prep habit for a busy family
description: A single parent is tired of expensive, unhealthy takeout on weeknights. They have a slow cooker but only use it for 'special occasions.' They want to build a routine where dinner is ready the moment they get home with the kids, reducing evening stress.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must leave the house by 7:30 AM; kids have soccer practice on Tuesdays and Thursdays.
baseline.physical_constraints: Morning 'brain fog' makes complex chopping difficult before 7 AM; grocery store is a 15-minute drive.
baseline.non_negotiables: Meals must cost less than $15 for 4 servings; must include at least one vegetable per meal.

tasks.description: Chop all vegetables and measure spices the night before. Store them in a single container so they can be dumped into the cooker in the morning in under 60 seconds.
Add the liquid, protein, and pre-prepped veggies to the slow cooker and set the timer. This must happen before the first cup of coffee is finished.
On Sundays, look at what is already in the pantry/freezer and choose 3 slow-cooker recipes for the week based on those ingredients to save money.
While cleaning up the current dinner, check the plan for tomorrow. If meat needs defrosting, move it to the fridge now.

goal: Prepare and run a successful garage sale to fund a vacation
description: The user has a basement full of clutter and a goal to raise $500 for a summer trip. They are worried about the weather, people haggling too aggressively, and the embarrassment of no one showing up.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Can only be held on a Saturday/Sunday; local ordinance requires a $5 permit.
baseline.physical_constraints: Requires moving heavy furniture to the driveway; limited signage allowed by the HOA.
baseline.non_negotiables: Everything not sold by 4 PM on Sunday must be taken immediately to a donation center (no 're-cluttering' the basement).

tasks.description: Group items by type (Kitchen, Toys, Tools) and place them in boxes. This makes pricing and setup much faster later.
Use pre-printed stickers. For small items, use 'bundle' pricing (e.g., 5 books for $1) to move volume. Research prices for any high-value electronics.
Post high-quality photos of the 'anchor' items (furniture/tools) on Facebook Marketplace and Craigslist 3 days before the sale.
Go to the bank for $50 in small bills and quarters; buy heavy-duty trash bags and permanent markers for the 'Half-Price' afternoon signs.
Load all unsold items into the car immediately at the end of day two. Do not bring them back into the house.

goal: Build a daily 'Nature Journaling' habit for stress reduction
description: The user is an amateur artist who feels 'blocked' and anxious. They want to use the local park as a sanctuary, but they get stuck on the idea that every drawing must be a masterpiece. They want to move from 'performing' art to 'experiencing' nature.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Sunsets early during the winter; park is safe only during daylight hours.
baseline.physical_constraints: Arthritis in fingers makes fine detail difficult in cold weather; requires portable, non-messy supplies.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be done outside (weather permitting); no social media posting of the results for the first 30 days.

tasks.description: Draw the first bird, tree, or cloud seen for exactly one minute. This breaks the 'blank page' fear by lowering the stakes to zero.
Instead of drawing, write down 3 things heard and 2 things smelled in the environment. This grounds the user when the 'artistic ego' gets too loud.
Every evening, ensure the sketchbook and pens are in the 'go-bag' by the door. If it's cold, pack hand-warmers and a thermos of tea.
Review the week's entries and add a single piece of tape or a sticker to the one that most reminds the user of how they felt, not how it looked.

goal: Decrease late-night social media reliance and improve sleep quality
description: A new parent relies on social media scrolling to stay awake during late-night infant feedings and to 'numb out' after stressful days. They want to break this cycle to improve their already fragmented sleep and reduce anxiety, but their extreme exhaustion makes it hard to resist the easy dopamine hit of the phone.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Infant wakes up 1-2 times a night; partner works early morning shifts so the user handles all night duties.
baseline.physical_constraints: Severe sleep deprivation significantly lowers impulse control and cognitive bandwidth.
baseline.non_negotiables: The phone must remain in the bedroom and un-silenced for the baby monitor app and pediatrician emergencies.

tasks.description: Configure the phone's accessibility settings to automatically turn the screen to grayscale at 8:00 PM. This makes the infinite feed visually unappealing without requiring daily willpower.

During night feedings, swap scrolling for listening to a familiar, low-stakes audiobook or podcast with a sleep timer. This keeps the user awake for the baby but allows the eyes to rest in a dark room.

Move the two most addictive social media apps to a hidden folder on the third page of the home screen, and delete the saved passwords. This forces the user to actively search and log in, interrupting the automatic 'muscle memory' of opening them.
When a doomscrolling relapse happens (which is expected), spend 2 minutes the next morning writing down what emotion triggered it (e.g., 'lonely,' 'overwhelmed') rather than engaging in self-guilt.

goal: Break the cycle of academic procrastination caused by short-form video apps
description: A high school student is experiencing a drop in grades because they spend 4-5 hours a day on TikTok and Instagram Reels. They experience intense FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out) and use the apps to cope with academic anxiety, but the avoidance only makes the anxiety worse.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Needs the internet and a laptop for homework; extracurricular activities end at 5 PM.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronically tired from staying up past midnight scrolling; easily distracted by notifications.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must keep messaging apps active for group project coordination and family communication.

tasks.description: Purchase and use a basic digital alarm clock. Keep the smartphone charging on a desk across the room during the night to prevent the 'just five minutes' morning scroll that turns into an hour.

Set a hard 30-minute daily limit on the top two distracting apps using the phone's native screen time tools. Give the override passcode to a parent or trusted friend so it cannot be bypassed in a moment of weakness.
Place the phone in another room while doing homework. Work in 25-minute bursts (Pomodoro technique) before allowing a 5-minute break. This builds focus endurance gradually.
Spend at least one hour on the weekend doing something completely offline and hands-on, like baking, drawing, or playing a sport, to re-sensitize the brain to slow-paced dopamine.

goal: Separate content creation from mindless consumption for business efficiency
description: A small business owner relies on social media for marketing, but opening the app to post a business update frequently leads to getting sucked into a 45-minute scroll of competitors' feeds. This leads to toxic comparison and lost billable hours.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must post content 3 times a week and reply to customer DMs within 24 hours.
baseline.physical_constraints: Stares at screens for 8+ hours a day, leading to decision fatigue by the afternoon.
baseline.non_negotiables: Cannot delete Instagram or Facebook; cannot hire a social media manager due to budget constraints.

tasks.description: Remove the social media apps from the mobile device and conduct all business posting and DM replies from a desktop web browser, which offers a clunkier, less addictive scrolling experience.
Dedicate one block of time on Monday to create, write, and schedule all posts for the week using a third-party scheduling tool. This eliminates the need to open the native apps daily.

If manual posting is required, set a timer for 10 minutes. Post the content, reply to 3 recent comments, and immediately close the tab when the timer goes off, refusing the urge to check the main feed.

Go through the following list and explicitly 'Mute' (without unfollowing) any accounts belonging to competitors that trigger imposter syndrome or anxiety. This cleanses the feed of negative emotional triggers.

goal: Reclaim evening connection by reducing 'phubbing' (phone-snubbing)
description: A couple has fallen into a routine where they spend their entire evening sitting on the same couch but silently scrolling on their respective phones. They feel distant from one another and often argue over minor miscommunications because neither is fully present.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Both partners work demanding full-time jobs and are mentally exhausted by 6 PM.
baseline.physical_constraints: Living room setup is oriented entirely around the TV and comfortable seating that encourages passive lounging.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be able to receive emergency texts from aging parents; neither partner can force the other to participate if they are too stressed.

tasks.description: Leave phones in a different room while eating dinner. Even if the meal is only 20 minutes long, this forces face-to-face interaction and breaks the continuous screen-staring cycle.

If one partner needs to zone out on their phone to decompress, they must verbally announce it (e.g., 'I need 20 minutes of brain-dead scrolling'). This removes the passive-aggressive tension of feeling ignored.

Keep a physical jigsaw puzzle or a low-stakes board game permanently set up on a small table. Spend 15 minutes working on it together twice a week while chatting, replacing screen time with collaborative play.
Spend 10 minutes on Sunday morning over coffee discussing what went well in the week and what felt disconnected. Keep the tone curious, not accusatory, regarding phone habits.

goal: Reduce 'doomscrolling' of news and political outrage content
description: A retiree has become addicted to arguing in comment sections on Facebook and endlessly refreshing news apps. This is causing chronic stress, elevating their blood pressure, and alienating family members who are tired of hearing them complain about the state of the world.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Has an abundance of free time; lives alone, making the internet their primary source of daily interaction.
baseline.physical_constraints: Mobility issues make leaving the house for long periods difficult.
baseline.non_negotiables: Wants to remain 'informed' about local and national events; will not delete Facebook entirely as it is their only link to grandchildren.

tasks.task: News App Consolidation and Notification Purge
tasks.description: Delete all but one reputable, neutral news app. Turn off all push notifications for breaking news so information is consumed intentionally rather than reactively.
Restrict news reading and political scrolling to a single 30-minute window in the morning with coffee. Once the cup is empty, close the apps for the remainder of the day.

When feeling the urge to argue with a stranger online, type the comment out in a private 'Notes' app to vent the frustration, then delete it instead of posting. This breaks the toxic feedback loop of internet arguments.
Whenever the urge to check the feed out of loneliness arises, call a friend or family member instead. Focus the conversation strictly on personal updates and shared memories, banning political talk.

goal: Execute a 14-day digital declutter to reset dopamine baseline
description: The user feels a profound lack of clarity and constant brain fog. They realize their phone is an appendage and want to do a 'hard reset' on their digital habits to re-evaluate what technologies actually add value to their life.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Needs Google Maps for commuting and Spotify for focus music during work.
baseline.physical_constraints: High probability of experiencing psychological 'withdrawal' symptoms (restlessness, anxiety) in the first 72 hours.
baseline.non_negotiables: Cannot ignore texts or calls from their spouse or boss.

tasks.description: Delete all social media, news, and non-essential entertainment apps from the smartphone. They can only be accessed via a desktop computer during the 14-day period.
Rearrange the phone's home screen so it only contains tools (Calendar, Maps, Weather, Notes). Remove the web browser from the main dock to prevent mindless searching.
Write down a physical list of 10 high-quality leisure activities (reading, walking, organizing a closet, calling a friend) and tape it to the fridge to reference when the 'itch' to scroll hits.
On day 7, write down which specific apps are genuinely missed because they provided real value, and which are only missed out of boredom or muscle memory. This guides what gets reinstalled later.
At the end of the 14 days, slowly reinstall only the 'valuable' apps one by one, establishing a specific rule for each (e.g., 'Instagram only on weekends').

goal: Stop using the phone as a psychological crutch in social situations
description: A young professional with mild social anxiety uses their phone as a 'shield' at parties, networking events, and bars. Whenever there is a lull in conversation, they immediately pull out their phone to look busy, which makes them unapproachable and deepens their feelings of isolation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Attends at least two mandatory networking or social events per week.
baseline.physical_constraints: Experiences elevated heart rate and sweaty palms when making small talk with strangers.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must keep the phone on vibrate in case their roommate gets locked out.

tasks.description: Upon arriving at an event, leave the phone in a coat pocket, purse, or backpack rather than keeping it in a pants pocket or hand. Physical distance reduces the reflexive urge to grab it during awkward silences.
Commit to speaking with one person or joining one group for at least three minutes before allowing a 'phone break.' Tolerate the initial discomfort of small talk without the digital escape hatch.

If the social overwhelm becomes too much, excuse yourself to the restroom for 5 minutes of scrolling to regulate your nervous system in private, rather than 'phubbing' people in the middle of the room.
On the commute home, write down the name of one new person you met or one interesting thing you learned. This reinforces the positive rewards of being present over the false comfort of the screen.

goal: Eliminate the 'dual-screening' habit to restore attention span
description: The user cannot watch a movie or TV show without simultaneously scrolling through TikTok or Twitter. They are frustrated that they can no longer follow complex plotlines and feel constantly overstimulated, yet find single-tasking 'boring' and under-stimulating.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Usually watches TV for 2 hours every evening to unwind from a stressful logistics job.
baseline.physical_constraints: Hands feel 'empty' and restless when not holding a device.
baseline.non_negotiables: Will not give up evening TV time, as it is a shared activity with their partner.

tasks.description: Before turning on the TV, plug the smartphone into a charger across the room. If the user wants to check the phone, they must physically stand up and walk to it.
Keep a physical object near the couch to satisfy the tactile craving. Use a stress ball, a sketchpad, or a simple puzzle ring to keep hands busy while keeping eyes on the television screen.
When the urge strikes to look up an actor or a fact about the show on the phone, write the question down on a sticky note instead. Wait until the episode is over to batch-search the answers.

Once a week, pick a complex, subtitle-heavy movie that demands full attention. Treat it as a 'workout' for the brain's attention span, practicing sitting through the slow parts without reaching for a digital distraction.

goal: Execute a 48-hour total digital detox weekend getaway
description: A chronically online marketing manager is facing severe burnout. They are planning a weekend cabin trip to disconnect completely, but they are terrified of being unreachable and fear they won't know how to relax without a feed to scroll.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Cabin is booked for a Friday night to Sunday afternoon; cell service is spotty anyway.
baseline.physical_constraints: High cortisol levels make sitting still and 'doing nothing' feel physically uncomfortable.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must give the cabin's landline number to their elderly parents for absolute emergencies.

tasks.description: Set an ironclad out-of-office email auto-responder and text close friends/family to inform them of the digital blackout. Distribute the cabin's emergency landline number.
Print out physical driving directions, the cabin access code, and recipes. Pack physical books, a deck of cards, and a disposable camera to replace the phone's utility functions.
Upon pulling into the cabin driveway, power down the smartphone entirely and lock it inside the car's glove compartment. Do not bring the device inside the living space.
When the inevitable anxiety of disconnection peaks (usually around Saturday morning), take a 30-minute walk in silence to process the discomfort rather than searching for a distraction.
On the drive home, turn the phone on but keep it on 'Do Not Disturb' mode. Only check messages once safely parked at home to avoid a sudden flood of cortisol from notifications.

goal: Purge and detoxify algorithmic feeds to reduce passive scrolling
description: The user realizes their social media feeds are filled with outrage-bait, 'rage-farming' influencers, and accounts that trigger body-image issues. They don't want to delete the apps entirely, but they want to consciously curate their digital environment so it is boring and useful rather than highly addictive.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Only has about 30 minutes of free time in the evenings to dedicate to this task.
baseline.physical_constraints: Gets easily fatigued by making too many micro-decisions in a row.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must stay followed to local community pages (town hall, local school) and immediate family members.

tasks.description: Go through the 'Following' list on the primary social app and unfollow at least 100 accounts. Focus ruthlessly on removing influencers, meme pages, and political commentators that incite anger or envy.
Navigate to the app's privacy/content settings and add 20 'muted words' related to triggering topics (e.g., politics, diet culture, celebrity gossip) to stop the algorithm from serving them.
Spend 15 minutes actively searching for and 'liking' mundane, calming content (e.g., woodworking, baking, nature photography) to force the algorithm to pivot away from high-arousal content.
Search 'unsubscribe' in the email inbox and systematically remove the address from 50 promotional lists that trigger the urge to mindlessly browse online shopping sites.

goal: Reclaim the first and last hour of the day from email and messaging
description: A remote tech worker has blurred the lines between work and home. They check Slack and email the moment they open their eyes in bed, and again right before sleeping. This causes their brain to always be in 'work mode,' leading to insomnia and morning anxiety.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Works with a global team across different time zones, so there are always messages waiting.
baseline.physical_constraints: Uses the phone as their primary morning alarm.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must be online and responsive by 9:00 AM local time for daily standup.

tasks.description: Set the phone's operating system to automatically enter a strict 'Sleep' mode from 9:30 PM to 8:00 AM, suppressing all work-related app notifications completely.

Complete showering, dressing, and eating breakfast before tapping the work communication apps. Treat the physical acts of the morning routine as a non-negotiable prerequisite to entering the digital office.

At 6:00 PM, send a final wrap-up message to the team, then forcefully close the Slack/Teams app on the phone. Speak the words 'I am done with work for the day' out loud to signal the brain to transition.
On Friday evenings, move all work-related communication apps into a buried folder on the last page of the home screen to prevent accidental, muscle-memory checking during the weekend.

goal: Eliminate smartphone distraction during dedicated parenting time
description: A parent feels guilty that they spend their time at the playground sitting on a bench scrolling through social media. They want to be more engaged and present, but they find repetitive toddler play boring and use the phone to cope with under-stimulation.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Visits the park for 1 hour every afternoon; must keep the phone nearby in case the other parent calls.
baseline.physical_constraints: Often physically tired after the workday, making running around difficult.
baseline.non_negotiables: Wants to be able to take occasional photos of the child; cannot leave the phone in the car for safety reasons.

tasks.description: Upon arriving at the park, immediately zip the smartphone into a hard-to-reach pocket of a backpack or diaper bag, rather than keeping it in a pants pocket.

Instead of scrolling while sitting, actively watch the child and internally narrate their actions or count how many times they go down the slide. This engages the brain without requiring high physical energy.
Commit to initiating at least one physical or imaginative game (like pushing the swing or playing 'monster') for a short burst before returning to the bench to rest.

If taking photos is desired, restrict it to the last 5 minutes of the park trip. Take the photos, put the phone immediately back in the bag, and do not look at them or post them until after the child is in bed.

goal: Reconfigure a smartphone interface to minimize ADHD doomscrolling loops
description: An adult with ADHD finds their phone to be a constant source of executive dysfunction. Bright red notification badges and infinite-scroll apps placed on the home screen cause them to lose hours of their day in 'time blindness.' They need to make the phone as boring and functional as a power tool.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Relies heavily on the phone's calendar widget and reminder apps to function at work.
baseline.physical_constraints: Easily overwhelmed by multi-step technical processes; tends to abandon projects halfway through.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must keep messaging apps visible for family, but all social media must be made difficult to access.

tasks.description: Go through the phone settings and manually turn off the red notification badges for every single app except direct messaging and missed phone calls. This stops the artificial urgency.
Remove all apps from the first and second home screens. Place a large Calendar widget on page one, and move all other apps into an alphabetized 'App Library' or hidden folder.
Set up an accessibility shortcut (like a triple-click of the power button) to instantly turn the screen black and white. This makes the phone instantly less stimulating when a doomscroll starts.
Install a third-party app blocker or use native screen time tools to require a passcode or FaceID to open any social media or news application, adding crucial seconds of friction.

goal: Reclaim a daily commute from passive scrolling for analog reading
description: A commuter spends 45 minutes each way on a train mindlessly swiping through short-form videos. They arrive at work feeling drained and agitated. They want to use this trapped time to read physical books, but the habit of pulling out the phone is deeply ingrained in their muscle memory.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Subway is often crowded, requiring the user to stand and hold a rail with one hand.
baseline.physical_constraints: Morning grogginess makes reading dense material difficult; holding a heavy hardcover is impractical while standing.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone to scan a digital transit pass at the entry and exit turnstiles.

tasks.description: Pack a lightweight paperback book or a dedicated e-reader into the outermost pocket of the work bag the night before. Ensure it is easier to reach than the smartphone.
Immediately after scanning the digital transit pass, put the phone into an interior zipped pocket of the bag before stepping onto the train platform. Do not hold it while waiting.
Once on the train, commit to reading just 10 pages. If the brain is too tired to continue after that, allow 'zoning out' or listening to ambient music, but do not return to social media.
On days when the train is too packed to physically hold a book, queue up an audiobook or an educational podcast before boarding, leaving the phone screen off for the duration of the ride.

goal: Eliminate social media scrolling during gym rest periods
description: A fitness enthusiast finds their 45-minute workouts are dragging into 90-minute sessions because they get sucked into Instagram between sets. Their heart rate drops, their muscles cool down, and they feel frustrated by their own lack of discipline, but staring at a blank gym wall feels awkwardly under-stimulating.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Needs to rest for exactly 60 to 90 seconds between heavy lifting sets.
baseline.physical_constraints: Sweaty hands make interacting with the phone screen annoying; the gym is highly stimulating and loud.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone to log weights and reps in a digital fitness tracker; streams music to wireless headphones.

tasks.description: Start the workout playlist and open the fitness tracking app before stepping onto the gym floor. Turn the phone onto 'Do Not Disturb' to prevent notification pop-ups from triggering a scroll.
Immediately after finishing a set, type the reps and weight into the tracker app, start the app's native 90-second rest timer, and hit the physical lock button to turn the screen black.
During the 90-second rest, replace the phone habit with a physical habit: take 5 deep diaphragmatic breaths, pace around the bench, or stretch the antagonist muscle group.
Compare the total workout duration at the end of the week. Seeing a 90-minute workout shrink back down to a dense, effective 50 minutes serves as positive reinforcement to maintain the boundary.

goal: Conduct a digital 'spring cleaning' of personal cloud storage and photos
description: The user receives daily warnings that their cloud storage is 99% full. They refuse to pay for a higher tier but are paralyzed by the thought of sorting through 20,000 photos and old screenshots. The digital clutter is causing low-level background anxiety and making it hard to find important documents.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Has roughly 2-3 hours on weekends to dedicate to this task.
baseline.physical_constraints: Eye strain from staring at thousands of tiny thumbnails.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must not accidentally delete any photos of family members or pets; cannot buy physical external hard drives.

tasks.description: Use the phone's native storage management tools to automatically identify and delete all video files larger than 100MB and all screenshots older than 6 months. This frees up immediate space with minimal decision fatigue.
Search the email inbox for 'unsubscribe' and bulk-delete thousands of old promotional emails, which silently take up significant shared cloud storage.

Instead of tackling the whole camera roll, select a specific year and month (e.g., Jan 2018). Spend 20 minutes deleting duplicates and blurry photos from just that one month. Stop when the timer goes off.
Log into the cloud drive on a desktop computer. Create three master folders ('Taxes/Admin', 'Personal Projects', 'Archive') and drag all loose, floating documents into their respective homes.
Review the 'Recently Deleted' folders across photos, emails, and cloud drives. Once confident, permanently empty the trash to finalize the storage reclamation.

goal: Eliminate smartphone use immediately upon waking up
description: The user starts every day by grabbing their phone from the nightstand and scrolling social media or email for 45 minutes in bed. This makes them chronically late, spikes their cortisol before they even stand up, and leaves them feeling reactive and rushed.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must wake up by 6:30 AM to catch the bus to work; partner sleeps until 7:00 AM.
baseline.physical_constraints: Morning grogginess makes willpower extremely low in the first 10 minutes of waking.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must check the weather before getting dressed; needs an alarm to wake up.

tasks.description: Plug the smartphone into a charger in the kitchen before going to the bedroom at night. This physically prevents the 'roll over and grab' muscle memory.
Set a traditional, standalone digital or mechanical alarm clock across the bedroom to wake up. This removes the excuse that 'the phone is my alarm.'
Immediately upon turning off the analog alarm, drink a full glass of water kept on the nightstand. This introduces a positive physical action to replace the dopamine hit of the screen.
Do not touch the smartphone in the kitchen until fully dressed and breakfast is started. Use a smart speaker or look out the window to check the weather.

goal: Reclaim the midday lunch break from passive doomscrolling
description: An office worker spends their entire 45-minute lunch break eating at their desk while scrolling through X (Twitter) and news sites. They return to work feeling unrefreshed, stiff, and intellectually drained by the negative content.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lunch break is exactly 45 minutes; office is located in a corporate park with limited walkable destinations.
baseline.physical_constraints: Experiences eye strain and neck pain from staring downward at a screen for 4+ hours before lunch.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must eat lunch (cannot skip it); cannot leave the immediate office building campus due to time constraints.

tasks.description: When leaving the desk to get lunch from the fridge or cafeteria, physically place the personal smartphone inside a drawer and close it. Do not take it to the eating area.
Eat the meal in the designated break room, an outdoor bench, or a cafeteria rather than the work desk. This creates a physical boundary between 'work/screen time' and 'recovery time.'
Bring a physical magazine, a Kindle (with wifi disabled), or a crossword puzzle to the break area to occupy the mind without the infinite algorithmic feed.
Spend the last 5 minutes of the break walking a lap around the office building or hallway to stretch the legs and reset the eyes' focal distance before returning to the computer screen.

goal: Establish a comprehensive 'Tech-Free Zone' in the physical home layout
description: A couple wants to redesign their living environment to naturally discourage phone usage because willpower alone isn't working. They realize that having chargers next to the couch and the bed makes it too easy to stay plugged in and scrolling for hours.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Living in a small apartment; limited available outlets.
baseline.physical_constraints: Couch is the primary place to relax after a long day of physical labor.
baseline.non_negotiables: Need a central place to charge phones where they can still hear a ringing phone if there is an emergency.

tasks.description: Unplug and remove all extra long phone charging cables from the living room side tables and bedroom nightstands. Put them in a storage bin.
Designate a single outlet in the hallway or kitchen as the 'Charging Hub.' Set up a multi-device charging block there, meaning devices must be left standing up and away from comfortable seating.
Place a high-quality art book, a magazine, or a knitting project on the living room coffee table exactly where the phones used to sit, creating an immediate physical alternative.
Create a small, aesthetic 'Phone Drop' bowl or basket at the entryway to act as a visual reminder to empty pockets and leave devices behind upon entering the home.

goal: Stop using the smartphone as an escape during bathroom breaks
description: The user frequently takes their phone into the bathroom and loses 20 to 30 minutes scrolling through TikTok or Instagram Shorts. This habit causes their legs to fall asleep, wastes valuable time in the morning, and makes them chronically late for commitments.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Shares a single bathroom with two roommates; mornings are highly time-sensitive.
baseline.physical_constraints: Prone to 'time blindness' and loses track of how long they have been sitting.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must maintain bathroom hygiene and free up the shared space quickly.

tasks.description: Before crossing the threshold of the bathroom door, physically place the phone face-down on a nearby desk, table, or bed. Never carry it into the room.
Keep a physical comic book, a Sudoku book, or a bottle of shampoo with interesting ingredients in the bathroom to satisfy the brain's craving for micro-stimulation without the infinite scroll.
Focus entirely on the necessary biological task. If boredom strikes, consciously remind yourself that it is acceptable for the brain to be entirely under-stimulated for five minutes.
When exiting the bathroom in 5 minutes instead of 25, take a mental note of the 'extra' 20 minutes recovered and use it for a slow stretch or making a better cup of coffee.

goal: Cultivate a 'Screen-Free' dog walking routine to practice mindfulness
description: A pet owner realizes they spend the entire 30-minute daily dog walk looking down at their phone, answering emails, or checking social media. They are missing their dog's training cues, nearly tripping on uneven sidewalks, and losing a prime opportunity for outdoor mental reset.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dog must be walked twice a day regardless of weather or mood.
baseline.physical_constraints: Holding a leash in one hand makes one-handed phone scrolling clumsy and drops likely.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must carry the phone for safety reasons (neighborhood is dark at night).

tasks.description: Right before clipping the leash onto the dog, swipe the phone into 'Do Not Disturb' mode. This pairs the physical action of preparing the dog with the digital boundary.
Place the phone in a zipped pocket or a fanny pack that requires two hands to open. Do not carry the phone in an open hand or a loose jacket pocket.
Use the hand that normally holds the phone to hold a clicker or a small bag of treats. Actively practice 'loose leash' training and reward the dog when they make eye contact.

During the walk, intentionally identify 5 things you can see (e.g., a specific tree), 3 things you can hear (e.g., distant traffic, birds), and 1 thing you can smell. This forces the attention outward rather than downward.

goal: Reclaim family dinner time from device distraction
description: A working parent constantly checks work emails and news during dinner, causing their children to mimic the behavior and bringing mealtime conversation to a halt. They want to create a sacred, device-free mealtime but feel anxious about missing urgent messages from their boss.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Dinner is rushed between 6:30 PM and 7:15 PM before the kids' bedtime routine begins.
baseline.physical_constraints: The kitchen island currently serves as the charging station for all family devices.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must have the phone ring loudly if an elderly parent calls, but no visual checking is allowed.

tasks.description: Move the multi-device charger from the kitchen island to a bedroom or office. This removes the visual cue and physical temptation to grab the phone while eating.
Ten minutes before serving dinner, everyone in the household must place their phone in a designated basket in the living room, ensuring devices are out of arm's reach.
Introduce one simple, non-confrontational question to the table (e.g., 'What was the weirdest part of your day?'). This fills the silence that the phone used to occupy.
Resist retrieving the phone from the basket until the table is entirely cleared and the dishwasher is loaded. This builds tolerance for delayed digital gratification.

goal: Eliminate the 'micro-boredom' scrolling habit while waiting in lines
description: A college student reflexively pulls out their phone the second they have to wait for anything—an elevator, a coffee, or a professor. This constant stream of 60-second stimulation is ruining their attention span for long lectures and making them highly impatient.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Spends at least 30 to 45 minutes a day in various queues or waiting periods on a crowded campus.
baseline.physical_constraints: Phone is habitually kept in the front pocket or carried directly in the hand.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone to scan digital ID for building access and to pay for cafeteria meals.

tasks.description: Keep the phone inside a zipped pocket of a backpack rather than a pants pocket. The physical friction of unzipping a bag deters mindless reaching.
When the urge to check the phone hits while waiting, take three slow, deep breaths before deciding if retrieving the phone is actually necessary or just a nervous tic.
Intentionally look around the environment while waiting. Read a bulletin board flyer, observe the architecture, or simply let the eyes rest by looking out a window.
When approaching a register, take the phone out exactly when the cashier asks for payment, complete the transaction, and immediately place it back in the bag without unlocking the home screen.

goal: Reduce 'second-screen' distraction during deep work blocks
description: A remote software developer struggles to focus on complex coding tasks. Every time the code is compiling or they hit a mental block, they grab their phone to check a forum, turning a 2-minute break into a 30-minute detour and killing their momentum.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must be at the computer for 8 hours a day; frequent 1-to-3 minute waiting periods while software compiles.
baseline.physical_constraints: Suffers from a stiff neck and eye strain from constant screen exposure.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must run an authenticator app on the phone to access company servers multiple times a day.

tasks.task: The 'Face Down, Out of Reach' Placement
tasks.description: After using the authenticator app, place the phone face down on a shelf or table that requires physically rolling the chair away from the desk to reach it.
Keep a physical pen and paper on the desk. When the urge to look up a random fact or check a website arises, write it down to research later rather than breaking focus immediately.
When the computer is loading or compiling, stand up and do a quick physical stretch (e.g., reaching for the ceiling or doing a torso twist) instead of reaching for the phone to fill the gap.
Allow 15 minutes of guilt-free, unrestricted scrolling at the very end of the workday as a reward for maintaining focus, completely satisfying the craving in a contained window.

goal: Establish a weekly 24-hour 'Tech Sabbath' to recover from burnout
description: A couple feels completely burnt out by Sunday evening because they spend the entire weekend passively consuming news and social media instead of resting. They want to turn Saturdays into a strict screen-free day, but struggle with the sudden boredom and the ingrained habit of checking devices.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Saturdays are the only days they have zero work obligations; Sundays are used for chores and meal prep.
baseline.physical_constraints: Prone to lying on the couch for hours if not prompted to move or engage in an activity.
baseline.non_negotiables: Can use a screen for a shared movie night on Saturday evening, but absolutely no individual scrolling or social media during the day.

tasks.description: At 9:00 PM on Friday, both partners physically power down their smartphones and place them in a drawer. This marks the official start of the Tech Sabbath and removes morning temptation.
Engage in a predetermined offline activity on Saturday morning, such as visiting a farmer's market, reading a physical book, or doing a puzzle, to prevent the 'what do we do now?' void.
When a lull in the day occurs, consciously sit with the feeling of boredom for at least 20 minutes without seeking an immediate escape. Allow the mind to wander and rest.
Before turning the phones back on Sunday morning, discuss over coffee how the disconnection felt. Note any improvements in sleep quality or connection to reinforce the habit for the next week.

goal: Reorganize the digital workspace to prevent accidental doomscrolling
description: An entrepreneur's phone is a chaotic mess of work apps and social media. They open their phone to check an invoice or reply to a client and end up watching videos for 40 minutes because the icons are right next to each other. They need to completely separate utility from entertainment.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Only has a few hours on a Sunday afternoon to completely overhaul their system.
baseline.physical_constraints: Experiences decision fatigue quickly when organizing digital files and settings.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must keep LinkedIn and Twitter installed for lead generation, but wants to stop mindless browsing on them during off-hours.

tasks.description: Clear the entire first page of the home screen. Populate it only with boring, highly functional tools: Calendar, Calculator, Maps, Notes, and Banking. This makes the phone act like a tool, not a toy.
Group all social media, news, and entertainment apps into a single folder. Move that folder to the third or fourth page of the home screen, requiring multiple swipes to access.
Go into the phone's settings and ruthlessly turn off notifications (banners, badges, and sounds) for everything except calendar events, texts, and phone calls.
Install the native screen-time tracking widget prominently on the second page of the home screen. This creates a visual point of accountability before swiping further to find entertainment apps.
Delete the native app for the most addictive platform (e.g., Reddit or TikTok) and force yourself to log in through the mobile web browser if you want to use it, adding intentional friction.

goal: Transition from watching hobby videos to actual physical creation
description: An amateur woodworker spends 2-3 hours an evening watching Instagram reels and YouTube tutorials about woodworking, but never actually goes out to the garage to build anything. They feel intimidated by the perfection of online creators and use video consumption as a false sense of productivity.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Only has free time after 8:00 PM when the kids are asleep; garage cannot be excessively loud after 9:30 PM.
baseline.physical_constraints: Often mentally fatigued from a desk job, making the physical barrier to entry feel high.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone for music/podcasts while working in the garage.

tasks.description: Print out physical blueprints or sketch designs on paper before going to the garage. This eliminates the need to unlock the phone to check measurements, which often triggers a scrolling spiral.
Start the playlist or podcast while still inside the house. Upon entering the garage, place the phone inside a closed toolbox drawer across the room so it is out of sight.
Commit to just 15 minutes of low-stakes physical activity, like sanding a scrap piece or organizing clamps. This overcomes the perfectionism barrier that drives the user back to watching videos.
Once a week, identify and unfollow one top-tier creator whose highly produced content triggers imposter syndrome rather than genuine inspiration.

goal: Reclaim the teacher planning period from passive scrolling
description: A high school teacher uses their 50-minute planning period to 'decompress' by scrolling TikTok, which leads to them bringing grading and lesson planning home every night. They feel overworked and resentful, but their brain craves the dopamine hit after dealing with noisy classrooms.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Planning period is strictly 50 minutes; must share an office with two other chatting teachers.
baseline.physical_constraints: Experiencing vocal fatigue and sensory overload by mid-day.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must check school email for urgent administrative updates during this block.

tasks.description: Spend exactly 5 minutes reviewing the school email inbox for emergencies. Reply to what is urgent, close the email tab, and immediately put the smartphone inside a zipped desk drawer.
Write the three most important tasks (e.g., 'Grade Quiz 4', 'Copy worksheets') on a physical sticky note and place it on the keyboard. Do not open the laptop until the list is written.
Put on noise-canceling headphones playing brown noise or instrumental music to block out the shared office chatter. Work continuously for 25 minutes using a physical desk timer.
Instead of using the remaining 10 minutes to scroll, leave the office without the phone and walk a lap around the quietest part of the school building to physically process the morning's stress.

goal: Prepare and execute a 72-hour strict digital detox for deep writing
description: An academic is severely behind on their thesis deadline because they constantly check Twitter and news sites under the guise of 'research.' They have booked a 3-day weekend alone to finally write the core chapters, but fear their addiction to distraction will ruin the retreat.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Only has 3 days to complete 10,000 words; location is a rented cabin with WiFi.
baseline.physical_constraints: Prone to tension headaches from marathon typing sessions.
baseline.non_negotiables: Requires the laptop for Microsoft Word; must check in with their partner once a day for safety.

tasks.description: Download all necessary PDF journals, datasets, and reference materials into a single local folder on the desktop. This eliminates the excuse of needing the internet to 'look something up.'
Upon arriving at the cabin, physically unplug the WiFi router and lock the power cord in the car trunk. The inconvenience of retrieving it creates a massive barrier to mindless browsing.
Power down the smartphone entirely. Move the SIM card into a pre-purchased basic 'dumb phone' that only has calling and texting capabilities for the duration of the retreat.
Call the partner at exactly 7:00 PM each night for 15 minutes. This satisfies the safety requirement without allowing continuous, day-long text message interruptions.
Keep a piece of paper beside the laptop. Every time the autonomic urge to open a new tab or check a feed arises, make a tally mark. Seeing the physical volume of urges helps demystify the addiction.

goal: Break the cycle of anxiety-driven parenting forum doomscrolling
description: A parent of a toddler is experiencing severe anxiety. They spend hours every evening reading parenting forums, Reddit threads, and milestone-tracking apps, comparing their child to others. This 'research' is intended to soothe anxiety but actually fuels panic and robs them of joyful presence.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Toddler has a speech delay, requiring actual legitimate research and medical coordination.
baseline.physical_constraints: Chronically exhausted, making the couch and the phone the path of least resistance at night.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone to communicate with the speech therapist via a secure medical app.

tasks.description: Delete all native forum apps (Reddit, Facebook, specific parenting apps). Bookmark only two evidence-based medical websites on the browser, preventing algorithmic 'rabbit holes.'
Restrict all child-development research to a single 20-minute window on Tuesday and Thursday afternoons. If a worry arises outside this window, write it in a notebook to check later.
Replace the evening scrolling habit with 10 minutes of writing in a physical journal. Focus entirely on one positive interaction or funny moment the child had that day, ignoring clinical milestones.
When the chest-tightening urge to Google a medical symptom hits, drop the phone and immediately do 10 wall push-ups or hold a piece of ice. Break the psychological panic loop with physical sensation.

goal: Eliminate smartphone usage while eating meals alone
description: A single professional living alone has developed a habit of propping their phone against a water glass and watching YouTube shorts or scrolling Instagram during every meal. They are eating too quickly, suffering from indigestion, and feeling entirely disconnected from the sensory experience of their food.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Eats all weekday breakfasts and dinners alone in their apartment.
baseline.physical_constraints: Silence in the apartment feels deafening and uncomfortable initially.
baseline.non_negotiables: Can listen to background music, but cannot look at any screens with moving images or text.

tasks.description: Before sitting down with the food, take the smartphone and plug it into a charger in a completely different room (e.g., the bedroom). Return to the kitchen table empty-handed.

If the silence is too anxiety-inducing, turn on a smart speaker or radio to play jazz, classical music, or a long-form interview podcast before sitting down. This provides stimulation without locking the eyes to a screen.
Eat the first three bites of the meal with eyes closed. Focus entirely on identifying the specific spices, textures, and temperatures of the food to anchor the brain in the physical present.

Once a baseline of tolerance for being screen-free is established, introduce a physical magazine or non-fiction book to the table for longer meals. This keeps the mind engaged without the addictive algorithmic loop.

goal: Break the cycle of social media comparison to cure creative block
description: An illustrator uses Instagram to promote their art, but opening the app to post turns into hours of scrolling other artists' work. This triggers severe imposter syndrome, making them feel their work isn't 'good enough,' leading to paralysis and avoiding the drawing tablet altogether.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must post to social media twice a week to maintain algorithm engagement for freelance commissions.
baseline.physical_constraints: Works alone in a home studio, making the phone the only source of 'social' interaction during the day.
baseline.non_negotiables: Cannot delete the app; relies on DMs for client inquiries.

tasks.description: Do not open any social media app until at least one hour of physical or digital sketching is completed. Establish that the brain must produce before it is allowed to consume.
When posting art, set a physical timer for 10 minutes. Upload the image, reply to recent comments, and close the app immediately when the timer sounds, without looking at the main feed.
Unfollow or mute artists whose work triggers anxiety or jealousy rather than genuine inspiration. Protect the digital environment so it serves as a tool, not a weapon against self-esteem.

Instead of using Pinterest or Instagram for reference images, spend time looking through physical art books or taking walks to photograph real-world textures, breaking the reliance on the screen for ideas.

goal: Execute a 30-day social media blackout for a major certification exam
description: A nursing student is one month away from their board exams. They are panic-studying but constantly checking TikTok and Snapchat to self-soothe their intense anxiety, which only fractures their focus and leaves them feeling more unprepared.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Must study 6-8 hours a day; exam date is immovable.
baseline.physical_constraints: High caffeine intake and stress are causing poor sleep, lowering daytime willpower.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must coordinate with a study group; needs YouTube for specific medical tutorial videos.

tasks.description: Post a final message on all social platforms explaining the 30-day absence for boards, and provide a phone number for urgent texts. This creates social accountability and relieves the fear of 'ghosting' friends.
Delete the apps from the phone. Change the passwords to a random string of characters, write it on a piece of paper, and give that paper to a roommate to hide until the exam is over.
Install a website blocker (like Cold Turkey or Freedom) on the laptop to block web versions of social media and news sites, while explicitly keeping educational domains whitelisted.
Plan specific, offline activities for 15-minute study breaks (e.g., stretching, petting a dog, making tea) to replace the reflex of picking up the phone when a textbook chapter gets difficult.

goal: Reduce mindless mobile gaming and increase daily mobility
description: An older adult has developed a severe habit of playing 'match-3' and digital slot games on their tablet for 4-5 hours a day. They justify it as 'keeping the brain sharp,' but it has led to a highly sedentary lifestyle, stiff joints, and neglecting household hobbies.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Lives in a climate with harsh winters, making outdoor walking difficult on some days.
baseline.physical_constraints: Mild arthritis makes prolonged standing uncomfortable; eyesight requires a large tablet screen rather than a phone.
baseline.non_negotiables: Uses the same tablet to video call grandchildren and read the morning news.

tasks.description: Move the tablet charger from the side table next to the favorite armchair to a kitchen counter. The tablet must remain plugged in the kitchen overnight and during the day when not actively used for a specific purpose.

If the user wants to play a game, they must do so while standing up at the kitchen counter. If their legs or back get tired, the gaming session must end. This limits sessions naturally without requiring complex app blockers.

Replace one hour of gaming with listening to an audiobook or a nostalgic radio show while slowly pacing the house or doing light stretches. This provides entertainment while stimulating physical blood flow.
Every Sunday, choose one game application that causes the most mindless 'zoning out' and permanently delete it. Slowly shrink the library of available distractions.

goal: Establish strict weekend boundaries for work-related phone checking
description: A mid-level manager is physically present at their children's weekend soccer games but mentally absent. They reflexively check Slack and email every 10 minutes, responding to non-urgent messages and blurring the lines between the office and home, causing resentment from their spouse.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Weekends are packed with family logistics and chores.
baseline.physical_constraints: The phone is used as the primary camera to take pictures of the kids playing sports.
baseline.non_negotiables: Cannot delete work apps because they are required on Monday mornings; must be reachable by text for family coordination.

tasks.description: At 6:00 PM on Friday, drag the work email and team messaging apps off the home screen and into a buried folder. Turn off notifications for these specific apps at the OS level until Monday morning.

During family events (like a soccer game), use the lock screen shortcut to access the camera. Take the photos, and physically lock the phone again without swiping up to see the home screen or notification center.

When at home on Saturday and Sunday, leave the phone on a high shelf in the kitchen. Wear a basic digital watch to check the time, removing the excuse of 'I just pulled it out to see what time it is.'

If weekend checking is driven by Monday anxiety, allow exactly 20 minutes on Sunday night after the kids are asleep to scan the inbox for true emergencies, write a to-do list, and then shut the phone off completely.

goal: Execute a tech-minimalist romantic vacation
description: A couple is going on an anniversary trip. On previous vacations, they spent hours in the hotel room scrolling social media, looking up restaurant reviews, and posting photos, which led to arguments about not being 'present.' They want to travel like it is 1999.

baseline.fixed_constraints: Trip lasts exactly 7 days in an unfamiliar city.
baseline.physical_constraints: Neither partner has a good sense of direction, leading to anxiety about getting lost without GPS.
baseline.non_negotiables: Must use the phone for mobile boarding passes and scanning museum tickets.

tasks.description: Before leaving, print out physical copies of the hotel confirmation, flight details, and a map of the destination city. Circle 3 potential restaurants near the hotel to avoid 'review paralysis' on the street.
Once the plane lands, switch both smartphones to Airplane Mode. Turn on Wi-Fi only for specific, necessary transactions (like calling a rideshare), then immediately revert to Airplane Mode.
Purchase a disposable camera, a Polaroid, or a basic point-and-shoot digital camera. Leave the phones in the hotel safe while sightseeing to eliminate the urge to instantly edit and upload photos.

Allow a strict 15-minute window in the evening, back at the hotel, to connect to Wi-Fi. Use this time to text family that you are safe and check for absolute emergencies. Do not open social media apps.
